<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create the demo runbook — a printable step-by-step cheat sheet for the presenter.

## File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\docs\demo_runbook.md

This is a concise, printable reference the presenter can keep beside them during the live demo. It should be scannable at a glance — NOT a detailed document.

### Format Guidelines:
- Use checkboxes for each step
- Keep instructions to ONE LINE per step where possible
- Bold the key action words (Click, Navigate, Type, Show, Say)
- Include exact URLs to open
- Include the "what to say" as brief quoted text
- Total length: ~150-200 lines max (fits on 3-4 printed pages)

### Content Structure:

```markdown
# Demo Runbook — Fabric CI/CD for Lakehouse Schema Evolution
> **Duration:** 60 minutes | **Audience:** Digital Realty engineering team
> **Presenter:** [Your Name] | **Date:** [Date]

## Pre-Demo Checklist
- [ ] 3 Fabric workspaces created (DigitalRealty-Dev, -UAT, -Prod)
- [ ] Deployment Pipeline configured
- [ ] Azure DevOps repo with demo files pushed
- [ ] GitHub repo with demo files pushed
- [ ] Dev workspace connected to Git
- [ ] Service Principal registered and configured
- [ ] Browser tabs pre-opened: Fabric portal, Azure DevOps, GitHub
- [ ] PowerPoint open on Slide 1

---

## Part 1: Slides (10 min)
- [ ] Present Slide 1 — SDLC Overview (2 min)
- [ ] Present Slide 2 — What Git Tracks (2 min)
- [ ] Present Slide 3 — The Schema Gap (3 min)
- [ ] Present Slide 4 — Schema-as-Code Solution (3 min)
- [ ] Present Slide 5 — Demo Roadmap

## Part 2: Environment Tour (8 min)
- [ ] **Show** Workspaces: Fabric portal → Workspaces → click each one
  > "3 workspaces mirroring our environments..."
- [ ] **Show** Deployment Pipeline: gear icon → Deployment pipelines → DigitalRealty-Pipeline
  > "Built-in pipeline to promote artifacts..."
- [ ] **Show** Azure DevOps repo: dev.azure.com → Repos → show folder structure
  > "Our migration scripts live here..."
- [ ] **Show** GitHub repo: github.com → same structure
- [ ] **Show** Git Integration: Dev workspace → Settings → Git integration
  > "Dev is connected to our Azure DevOps repo..."

## Part 3: Happy Path (8 min)
[... continue with concise steps for Parts 3-6 ...]

## Part 6: REST APIs (8 min)
[... REST API demo steps ...]

## Wrap-Up (3 min)
- [ ] Summarize: Schema-as-Code + CI/CD + REST APIs = full coverage
- [ ] Show the architecture slide one more time
- [ ] Open for Q&A
```

Include ALL 6 parts with concise actionable steps. Include the key "what to say" quotes. Add timing markers. Make it practical and usable.

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'write-runbook';
```

</details>

Background agent "Write data quality checks" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create a data quality checks notebook for the Fabric CI/CD demo.

## File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\notebooks\data_quality_checks.py

This is a Fabric notebook that validates schema after migration. It's run as a post-migration step by the CI/CD pipeline.

### Parameters:
```python
# Parameters
target_environment = "Dev"  # Dev, UAT, or Prod
expected_version = "v002"   # Expected migration version
```

### Checks to implement:

1. **Table Existence Check** — Verify all expected tables exist:
   - v001 tables: data_centers, power_consumption, cooling_metrics
   - v002 tables: capacity_utilization, sla_incidents
   - Use `spark.catalog.tableExists()` or `spark.sql("SHOW TABLES")`
   - Print ✅ or ❌ for each table

2. **Column Validation** — For each table, verify expected columns exist with correct types:
   - Use `spark.sql("DESCRIBE TABLE tablename")` 
   - Compare against expected schema dictionary
   - Print ✅ or ❌ for each column check

3. **v002-specific checks** — If expected_version >= v002:
   - Check power_consumption has renewable_pct and carbon_intensity_kg columns
   - Check capacity_utilization and sla_incidents tables exist

4. **Row Count Check** — Verify tables are not empty (if sample data was inserted):
   - Print row counts for each table

5. **Data Integrity Checks:**
   - Check for null values in key columns (dc_id, record_id, incident_id)
   - Check PUE ratio is within valid range (1.0 - 3.0)
   - Check utilization_pct is between 0 and 100

6. **Migration History Check** — Verify _migration_history table exists and has expected records

### Summary:
- At the end, print a summary report:
  ```
  ═══════════════════════════════════════
  Schema Validation Report — {target_environment}
  ═══════════════════════════════════════
  Tables:     5/5 ✅
  Columns:    28/28 ✅
  Data:       All checks passed ✅
  Migration:  v002 applied ✅
  ═══════════════════════════════════════
  RESULT: PASS ✅
  ```
- If any check fails, the summary should say FAIL ❌ and the notebook should raise an exception (so the CI/CD pipeline detects the failure)

Use clear section headers, emoji status indicators, and descriptive print statements for the demo.

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'write-quality-checks';
```

</details>

Background agent "Write PySpark migration scripts" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create two PySpark migration scripts for a Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse demo. These are the PySpark equivalents of SQL migration scripts. The data domain is "data center operations" for Digital Realty.

## File 1: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\lakehouse-migrations\migrations\v001_baseline_tables.py

Create 3 baseline tables using PySpark/Delta Lake. This is a Fabric Notebook cell script. Use `spark.sql()` for DDL where simple, and DeltaTable API where it adds value.

Tables (same schema as SQL version):

1. `data_centers` — dc_id STRING, dc_name STRING, region STRING, city STRING, country STRING, total_capacity_kw DOUBLE, commissioned_date DATE, tier_level INT, status STRING

2. `power_consumption` — record_id STRING, dc_id STRING, measurement_timestamp TIMESTAMP, total_power_kw DOUBLE, it_load_kw DOUBLE, cooling_load_kw DOUBLE, lighting_load_kw DOUBLE, pue_ratio DOUBLE

3. `cooling_metrics` — record_id STRING, dc_id STRING, measurement_timestamp TIMESTAMP, supply_temp_celsius DOUBLE, return_temp_celsius DOUBLE, humidity_pct DOUBLE, chiller_efficiency DOUBLE, cooling_method STRING

Requirements:
- Use `spark.sql("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ...")` for table creation
- After creating each table, print a success message like: `print("✅ Created table: data_centers")`
- Add sample data insertion (3-5 rows per table) using `spark.sql("INSERT INTO ...")` with realistic data center data
- Wrap everything in try/except with clear error messages
- Add a module docstring at the top explaining this is migration v001

## File 2: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\lakehouse-migrations\migrations\v002_capacity_and_sustainability.py

Schema evolution using PySpark:

1. Create `capacity_utilization` table (same columns as SQL version)
2. Create `sla_incidents` table (same columns as SQL version)  
3. ALTER TABLE power_consumption ADD COLUMNS (renewable_pct DOUBLE, carbon_intensity_kg DOUBLE)
4. Insert sample data into new tables

Same patterns: spark.sql(), try/except, print status messages, CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS.

After creating both files, update the todo status:
```sql

```

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 23m 14s</sub>

### ℹ️ Notification

Background agent "Build migration runner notebook" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create the migration runner notebook for Microsoft Fabric. This is the core engine that applies schema migrations to a Lakehouse.

## File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\lakehouse-migrations\migration_runner.py

This is a parameterized Fabric notebook. Requirements:

### Parameters (at the top, with defaults):
```python
# Parameters - these are set by the CI/CD pipeline or manually
target_environment = "Dev"       # Dev, UAT, or Prod
migration_type = "sql"           # sql or pyspark
migrations_path = "/lakehouse/default/Files/migrations"  # path in Lakehouse Files
dry_run = False                  # If True, just shows what would run
```

### Logic Flow:

1. **Initialize** — Print banner with target environment and migration type
2. **Create migration state table** if it doesn't exist:
   ```sql
   CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _migration_history (
       migration_id STRING,
       migration_name STRING,
       applied_at TIMESTAMP,
       applied_by STRING,
       migration_type STRING,
       status STRING,
       duration_seconds DOUBLE
   )
   ```
3. **Discover available migrations** — List all files in migrations_path matching the migration_type (*.sql or *.py), sorted by version number
4. **Check which migrations have been applied** — Query _migration_history for completed migrations
5. **Determine pending migrations** — Find migrations not yet applied
6. **Apply each pending migration:**
   - For SQL: Read the .sql file content and execute via `spark.sql()`
   - For PySpark: Read the .py file and execute via `exec()`
   - Record start time, execute, record end time
   - On success: Insert record into _migration_history with status='success'
   - On failure: Insert record with status='failed', print error, stop execution
7. **Print summary** — Number of migrations applied, total time, list of applied migrations

### Key Design Principles:
- **Idempotent** — Safe to run multiple times
- **Ordered** — Migrations run in version order (v001 before v002)
- **Auditable** — Every run is logged in _migration_history
- **Parameterized** — Can target any environment
- **Dry-run capable** — Can preview without executing

Add generous comments explaining each section. Use emoji in print statements for visual clarity (✅ ❌ ⏳ 📋).

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'build-migration-runner';
```

</details>

Background agent "Generate PowerPoint slide deck" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Generate a PowerPoint slide deck for the Fabric CI/CD demo using the python-pptx library.

## Output File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\presentation\Fabric_CICD_Demo.pptx

First install python-pptx: `pip install python-pptx`

Then create a Python script and run it to generate the .pptx file. The script can be temporary or saved as `C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\presentation\generate_slides.py`.

### Slide Design:
- Use a clean professional theme
- Microsoft blue color palette (primary: #0078D4, dark: #002050, accent: #50E6FF)
- White backgrounds with blue accents
- Clear, large fonts (titles 32pt+, body 18pt+)

### 5 Slides:

**Slide 1: "Microsoft Fabric — SDLC & CI/CD Overview"**
- Subtitle: "Automated deployment across Dev → UAT → Prod"
- Key bullet points:
  - Fabric Workspaces as environments
  - Git Integration for version control
  - Deployment Pipelines for promotion
  - REST APIs for automation
- Footer: "Digital Realty — Fabric CI/CD Workshop"

**Slide 2: "What Git Integration Tracks"**
- Two-column layout (use a table or text boxes):
  - Left column (✅ Tracked): Notebooks, Data Pipelines, Semantic Models, Reports/Dashboards, Spark Job Definitions, ML Models
  - Right column (❌ Not Tracked): Lakehouse Tables, Table Schemas & DDL, Views & Stored Procedures, Data Files, SQL Endpoint Objects
- Footer note: "Source: Microsoft Learn — Lakehouse Git Integration"

**Slide 3: "The Schema Gap"**
- Visual flow diagram using text boxes and shapes:
  - Dev Lakehouse (with tables icon) → Git (no tables) → UAT Lakehouse (empty)
- Key point: "Schema changes made in Dev Lakehouse do NOT propagate through Git or Deployment Pipelines"
- Use red/warning colors for the gap

**Slide 4: "The Solution — Schema-as-Code"**
- Architecture diagram showing the solution:
  - Migration scripts in Git → CI/CD Pipeline triggers → Migration Runner notebook executes in each environment
- Bullet points:
  - Version-controlled SQL & PySpark migration scripts
  - Idempotent migration runner notebook
  - Automated CI/CD pipeline (Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions)
  - Audit trail via _migration_history table
- Use green/success colors

**Slide 5: "Demo Roadmap"**
- Numbered list of what will be demonstrated:
  1. Environment Setup (3 workspaces + Deployment Pipeline)
  2. Standard CI/CD Flow (notebooks through Git)
  3. The Problem (tables don't propagate)
  4. Schema-as-Code Solution (migration scripts + runner)
  5. CI/CD Pipeline Automation (Azure DevOps + GitHub Actions)
  6. REST API Deep Dive (live API calls)
- "Let's see it in action! →"

### Architecture Diagrams:
For slides 3 and 4, create visual diagrams using python-pptx shapes:
- Use rectangles for boxes (workspaces, repos)
- Use arrows (lines with arrowheads) for flow
- Use colors: blue for Fabric items, gray for Git, green for solution, red for gaps
- Add text labels inside/above shapes

Make sure the .pptx file is saved to the correct path. After creating the file, verify it exists.

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql

```

<sub>⏱️ 23m 50s</sub>

### ℹ️ Notification

Background agent "Build GitHub Actions workflow" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create the GitHub Actions workflow for the Fabric CI/CD demo.

## File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\pipelines\.github\workflows\deploy-schema.yml

This workflow automates Lakehouse schema migration deployment, mirroring the Azure DevOps pipeline but using GitHub Actions syntax.

### Workflow Structure:

```yaml
name: Deploy Lakehouse Schema Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'lakehouse-migrations/**'
  workflow_dispatch:  # Allow manual trigger

env:
  FABRIC_API_BASE: https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1
```

### Jobs:

**Job 1: validate**
- Checkout code
- List changed migration files
- Basic syntax validation

**Job 2: deploy-uat** (needs: validate)
- Environment: `UAT` (for protection rules)
- Steps:
  1. Get Azure AD access token using secrets (`${{ secrets.FABRIC_TENANT_ID }}`, `${{ secrets.FABRIC_CLIENT_ID }}`, `${{ secrets.FABRIC_CLIENT_SECRET }}`)
  2. Trigger migration_runner notebook in UAT workspace via Fabric REST API
  3. Poll for job completion with timeout
  4. Run data quality checks

**Job 3: deploy-prod** (needs: deploy-uat)
- Environment: `Production` (for manual approval via GitHub Environment protection rules)
- Same steps as UAT but targeting Prod workspace using `${{ secrets.PROD_WORKSPACE_ID }}`

### Key differences from Azure DevOps version:
- Use `${{ secrets.VAR_NAME }}` instead of `$(VAR_NAME)`
- Use `environment:` for approval gates (GitHub Environments with required reviewers)
- Use `needs:` for job dependencies
- Use bash `run:` blocks for REST API calls

Add clear comments, use descriptive step names, include error handling in the bash scripts. Add a comment at the top explaining that GitHub Environment protection rules must be configured for manual approvals.

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'build-github-workflow';
```

</details>

---

### ℹ️ Notification

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create two Python scripts for the Fabric REST API demo section.

## File 1: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\scripts\fabric_api_helper.py

A comprehensive Fabric REST API helper class. This will be shown during the demo and used in notebooks.

```python
"""
Fabric REST API Helper
Demonstrates key Fabric REST API operations for CI/CD automation.
Designed to be used in Fabric notebooks or standalone Python scripts.
"""
```

### Class: FabricApiClient

**Constructor:**
- tenant_id, client_id, client_secret (Service Principal credentials)
- base_url = "https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1"

**Methods:**

1. `get_access_token()` — Authenticate with Azure AD, return bearer token. Use requests library to POST to `https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant_id}/oauth2/v2.0/token` with `client_credentials` grant type and `https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/.default` scope.

2. `list_workspaces()` — GET /v1/workspaces, return list of workspaces with id, displayName, type

3. `get_workspace_items(workspace_id)` — GET /v1/workspaces/{id}/lakehouses (and notebooks), return items

4. `trigger_notebook_run(workspace_id, notebook_id, parameters=None)` — POST /v1/workspaces/{id}/items/{notebookId}/jobs/instances?jobType=RunNotebook, with optional execution parameters in the body

5. `poll_job_status(workspace_id, job_id, timeout=600, interval=15)` — Poll job status until complete or timeout. Return final status.

6. `git_connect(workspace_id, git_provider_details)` — POST /v1/workspaces/{id}/git/connect

7. `git_commit(workspace_id, commit_message, items=None)` — POST /v1/workspaces/{id}/git/commitToGit

8. `git_update_from_git(workspace_id)` — POST /v1/workspaces/{id}/git/updateFromGit

9. `deploy_pipeline(pipeline_id, source_stage, target_stage, items=None)` — POST /v1/deploymentPipelines/{id}/deploy

10. `list_deployment_pipelines()` — GET /v1/deploymentPipelines

### Helper:
- `_make_request(method, endpoint, **kwargs)` — Common method handling auth headers, error checking, retries
- All methods should have clear docstrings
- Print formatted JSON responses for demo visibility
- Include error handling with descriptive messages

### Main block:
```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Demo usage - shows each API call
    import os
    client = FabricApiClient(
        tenant_id=os.getenv("FABRIC_TENANT_ID", "<your-tenant-id>"),
        client_id=os.getenv("FABRIC_CLIENT_ID", "<your-client-id>"),
        client_secret=os.getenv("FABRIC_CLIENT_SECRET", "<your-client-secret>")
    )
    
    print("=== Listing Workspaces ===")
    workspaces = client.list_workspaces()
    for ws in workspaces:
        print(f"  {ws['displayName']} ({ws['id']})")
    # ... more examples
```

## File 2: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\scripts\setup_workspaces.py

A script that demonstrates how to provision and configure workspaces via the API. Less critical but nice for the demo.

### Contents:
- Import FabricApiClient from fabric_api_helper
- Function: `setup_demo_environment()` that:
  1. Lists existing workspaces
  2. Shows how you would create workspaces (with commented-out POST calls since workspace creation may require admin)
  3. Shows how to connect a workspace to Git
  4. Shows how to configure deployment pipeline stages
- Print each step with clear labels

After creating both files, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'write-api-scripts';
```

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 25m 55s</sub>

### ℹ️ Notification

Background agent "Build Azure DevOps pipeline" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

Create the Azure DevOps YAML pipeline for the Fabric CI/CD demo.

## File: C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\pipelines\azure-pipelines.yml

This pipeline automates Lakehouse schema migration deployment across UAT and Prod environments using Microsoft Fabric REST APIs.

### Pipeline Structure:

```yaml
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
  paths:
    include:
      - lakehouse-migrations/**

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

variables:
  - name: tenantId
    value: '$(FABRIC_TENANT_ID)'        # Set in pipeline variables
  - name: clientId
    value: '$(FABRIC_CLIENT_ID)'        # Service Principal app ID
  - name: clientSecret
    value: '$(FABRIC_CLIENT_SECRET)'    # Service Principal secret
  - name: uatWorkspaceId
    value: '$(UAT_WORKSPACE_ID)'        # Fabric workspace GUID
  - name: prodWorkspaceId
    value: '$(PROD_WORKSPACE_ID)'       # Fabric workspace GUID
  - name: migrationNotebookId
    value: '$(MIGRATION_NOTEBOOK_ID)'   # migration_runner notebook item ID
```

### Stages:

**Stage 1: Validate**
- Checkout code
- Validate that migration SQL files have valid syntax (basic lint - check for CREATE TABLE, no DROP DATABASE, etc.)
- List new/changed migration files

**Stage 2: Deploy to UAT**
- Step 1: Get Azure AD token using Service Principal credentials (use `curl` to call `https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenantId}/oauth2/v2.0/token` with scope `https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/.default`)
- Step 2: Trigger the migration_runner notebook in UAT workspace via Fabric REST API:
  ```
  POST https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{uatWorkspaceId}/items/{migrationNotebookId}/jobs/instances?jobType=RunNotebook
  ```
  With request body specifying parameters: `target_environment=UAT`, `migration_type=sql`
- Step 3: Poll for job completion (GET the job status URL until complete)
- Step 4: Run data quality validation (trigger quality check notebook)

**Stage 3: Deploy to Prod** (with manual approval)
- Add an environment with manual approval gate: `environment: 'Production'`
- Same steps as UAT but with prodWorkspaceId and target_environment=Prod

### Important Notes:
- Use `displayName` for each step to make the pipeline readable
- Add comments explaining each section
- Use bash scripts for the REST API calls
- Include error handling (check HTTP status codes)
- Add a timeout for job polling (max 10 minutes)
- Mark secrets with `isSecret: true` where appropriate
- Add a header comment block explaining what this pipeline does

After creating the file, update the todo status:
```sql
UPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'build-azdo-pipeline';
```

</details>

1. from pathlib import Path
2. 
3. from pptx import Presentation
4. from pptx.dml.color import RGBColor
5. from pptx.enum.shapes import MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE, MSO_CONNECTOR
6. from pptx.enum.text import MSO_VERTICAL_ANCHOR, PP_ALIGN
7. from pptx.util import Inches, Pt
8. 
9. PRIMARY_BLUE = RGBColor(0x00, 0x78, 0xD4)
10. DARK_BLUE = RGBColor(0x00, 0x20, 0x50)
11. ACCENT_CYAN = RGBColor(0x50, 0xE6, 0xFF)
12. SUCCESS_GREEN = RGBColor(0x10, 0x7C, 0x10)
13. LIGHT_GREEN = RGBColor(0xE7, 0xF4, 0xE4)
14. WARNING_RED = RGBColor(0xD1, 0x34, 0x38)
15. LIGHT_RED = RGBColor(0xFE, 0xF0, 0xF1)
16. GRAY = RGBColor(0x60, 0x60, 0x60)
17. LIGHT_GRAY = RGBColor(0xE8, 0xEA, 0xED)
18. WHITE = RGBColor(0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF)
19. BLACK = RGBColor(0x20, 0x20, 0x20)
20. FONT_NAME = "Aptos"
21. 
22. OUTPUT_PATH = Path(r"C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\presentation\Fabric_CICD_Demo.pptx")
23. 
24. 
25. 
26. def set_slide_background(slide, color=WHITE):
27.     fill = slide.background.fill
28.     fill.solid()
29.     fill.fore_color.rgb = color
30. 
31. 
32. 
33. def add_accent_line(slide):
34.     line = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, Inches(0.5), Inches(1.08), Inches(12.3), Inches(0.08))
35.     line.fill.solid()
36.     line.fill.fore_color.rgb = PRIMARY_BLUE
37.     line.line.fill.background()
38. 
39. 
40. 
41. def add_title(slide, title, subtitle=None):
42.     title_box = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(0.55), Inches(0.3), Inches(11.6), Inches(0.6))
43.     tf = title_box.text_frame
44.     tf.word_wrap = True
45.     p = tf.paragraphs[0]
46.     run = p.add_run()
47.     run.text = title
48.     run.font.name = FONT_NAME
49.     run.font.size = Pt(32)
50.     run.font.bold = True
51.     run.font.color.rgb = DARK_BLUE
52. 
53.     if subtitle:
54.         subtitle_box = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(0.6), Inches(1.18), Inches(11.2), Inches(0.45))
55.         stf = subtitle_box.text_frame
56.         sp = stf.paragraphs[0]
57.         srun = sp.add_run()
58.         srun.text = subtitle
59.         srun.font.name = FONT_NAME
60.         srun.font.size = Pt(20)
61.         srun.font.color.rgb = PRIMARY_BLUE
62. 
63.     add_accent_line(slide)
64. 
65. 
66. 
67. def set_shape_style(shape, fill_color=None, line_color=None, line_width=1.5):
68.     if fill_color is None:
69.         shape.fill.background()
70.     else:
71.         shape.fill.solid()
72.         shape.fill.fore_color.rgb = fill_color
73.     if line_color is None:
74.         shape.line.fill.background()
75.     else:
76.         shape.line.color.rgb = line_color
77.         shape.line.width = Pt(line_width)
78. 
79. 
80. 
81. def add_text_in_shape(shape, lines, font_size=18, color=BLACK, bold_first=False, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER):
82.     tf = shape.text_frame
83.     tf.clear()
84.     tf.word_wrap = True
85.     tf.vertical_anchor = MSO_VERTICAL_ANCHOR.MIDDLE
86.     tf.margin_left = Pt(8)
87.     tf.margin_right = Pt(8)
88.     tf.margin_top = Pt(6)
89.     tf.margin_bottom = Pt(6)
90. 
91.     for idx, text in enumerate(lines):
92.         p = tf.paragraphs[0] if idx == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
93.         p.alignment = align
94.         run = p.add_run()
95.         run.text = text
96.         run.font.name = FONT_NAME
97.         run.font.size = Pt(font_size)
98.         run.font.color.rgb = color
99.         run.font.bold = bold_first and idx == 0
100.         if idx > 0:
101.             p.space_before = Pt(2)
102. 
103. 
104. 
105. def add_bullets(slide, items, left, top, width, height, font_size=20, color=BLACK):
106.     box = slide.shapes.add_textbox(left, top, width, height)
107.     tf = box.text_frame
108.     tf.clear()
109.     tf.word_wrap = True
110.     tf.margin_left = Pt(6)
111.     tf.margin_right = Pt(6)
112.     for idx, item in enumerate(items):
113.         p = tf.paragraphs[0] if idx == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
114.         p.text = f"• {item}"
115.         p.font.name = FONT_NAME
116.         p.font.size = Pt(font_size)
117.         p.font.color.rgb = color
118.         p.space_after = Pt(8)
119.     return box
120. 
121. 
122. 
123. def add_footer(slide, text, color=GRAY):
124.     footer = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(0.6), Inches(7.0), Inches(12.0), Inches(0.3))
125.     tf = footer.text_frame
126.     p = tf.paragraphs[0]
127.     p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER
128.     run = p.add_run()
129.     run.text = text
130.     run.font.name = FONT_NAME
131.     run.font.size = Pt(11)
132.     run.font.color.rgb = color
133. 
134. 
135. 
136. def add_labeled_panel(slide, left, top, width, height, title, title_fill, border_color, items, symbol):
137.     header = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, left, top, width, Inches(0.55))
138.     set_shape_style(header, title_fill, title_fill)
139.     add_text_in_shape(header, [title], font_size=22, color=WHITE, bold_first=True)
140. 
141.     body = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, left, top + Inches(0.62), width, height - Inches(0.62))
142.     set_shape_style(body, WHITE, border_color)
143. 
144.     text_items = [f"{symbol} {item}" for item in items]
145.     add_bullets(slide, text_items, left + Inches(0.15), top + Inches(0.78), width - Inches(0.3), height - Inches(0.9), font_size=19, color=DARK_BLUE)
146. 
147. 
148. 
149. def add_arrow(slide, x1, y1, x2, y2, color, width=2.5):
150.     arrow_head = Inches(0.18)
151.     connector = slide.shapes.add_connector(MSO_CONNECTOR.STRAIGHT, x1, y1, x2 - arrow_head // 2, y2)
152.     connector.line.color.rgb = color
153.     connector.line.width = Pt(width)
154. 
155.     head = slide.shapes.add_shape(
156.         MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ISOSCELES_TRIANGLE,
157.         x2 - arrow_head,
158.         y2 - arrow_head // 2,
159.         arrow_head,
160.         arrow_head,
161.     )
162.     head.rotation = 90
163.     set_shape_style(head, color, color)
164.     return connector
165. 
166. 
167.

172. 
173.     banner = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, Inches(8.5), Inches(1.55), Inches(3.9), Inches(0.65))

175.     add_text_in_shape(banner, ["Digital Realty Demo"], font_size=22, color=DARK_BLUE, bold_first=True)

178.         "Fabric Workspaces as environments",
179.         "Git Integration for version control",
180.         "Deployment Pipelines for promotion",
181.         "REST APIs for automation",
182.     ]

184. 
185.     panel = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, Inches(7.8), Inches(2.35), Inches(4.2), Inches(2.75))

188.         panel,

190.             "Core Flow",

195.         ],
196.         font_size=19,
197.         color=DARK_BLUE,
198.         bold_first=True,

201.

228.     add_labeled_panel(slide, Inches(6.9), Inches(1.7), Inches(5.7), Inches(4.6), "❌ Not Tracked", WARNING_RED, WARNING_RED, not_tracked, "❌")
229.

244.

268.     )

276.     cr.font.name = FONT_NAME
277.     cr.font.size = Pt(18)
278.     cr.font.color.rgb = GRAY

321.     set_slide_background(slide)
322.     add_title(slide, "Demo Roadmap")
323.

329.         "5. CI/CD Pipeline Automation (Azure DevOps + GitHub Actions)",
330.         "6. REST API Deep Dive (live API calls)",

333.

335.     set_shape_style(callout, ACCENT_CYAN, PRIMARY_BLUE)
336.     add_text_in_shape(callout, ["Let's see it in action! →"], font_size=22, color=DARK_BLUE, bold_first=True)
337. 
338. 
339. 
340. def build_presentation():
341.     prs = Presentation()
342.     prs.slide_width = Inches(13.333)
343.     prs.slide_height = Inches(7.5)
344.     prs.core_properties.title = "Fabric CI/CD Demo"
345.     prs.core_properties.subject = "Microsoft Fabric SDLC & CI/CD Overview"
346.     prs.core_properties.author = "GitHub Copilot CLI"
347.     prs.core_properties.company = "Digital Realty"
348. 
349.     add_slide_one(prs)
350.     add_slide_two(prs)
351.     add_slide_three(prs)
352.     add_slide_four(prs)
353.     add_slide_five(prs)
354. 
355.     OUTPUT_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
356.     prs.save(OUTPUT_PATH)
357.     return OUTPUT_PATH
358. 
359. 
360. 
361. if __name__ == "__main__":
362.     output = build_presentation()
363.     print(f"Created presentation: {output}")
364.
```

C:\Users\sautalwar\.copilot\session-state\d91b6efe-a7c4-4a6c-a442-2561ba6f7b2c\plan.md (lines 1-50)

<details>
<summary>50 lines</summary>

```
1. # Demo Plan: Fabric CI/CD with Lakehouse Schema Evolution for Digital Realty
2. 
3. ## What This Demo Is About (Plain English)
4. 
5. Digital Realty uses Microsoft Fabric and has 3 environments: **Dev → UAT → Prod**. They want changes to flow automatically through all 3. Most Fabric items (notebooks, pipelines, reports) work great with Git — but **Lakehouse table schemas do NOT get tracked by Git**. So if someone creates a table in Dev, it doesn't automatically appear in UAT or Prod. This demo shows how to solve that.
6. 
7. ## What We Will Build
8. 
9. | # | Artifact | What It Is |
10. |---|----------|-----------|
11. | 1 | **PowerPoint slide deck** (.pptx) | 5 context slides to present before the live demo |
12. | 2 | **SQL migration scripts** | SQL files that create/alter Lakehouse tables |
13. | 3 | **PySpark migration scripts** | Python files that do the same thing using Spark |
14. | 4 | **Migration runner notebook** | A Fabric notebook that reads and runs migration scripts |
15. | 5 | **Azure DevOps YAML pipeline** | CI/CD pipeline that auto-deploys schema changes |
16. | 6 | **GitHub Actions workflow** | Same CI/CD pipeline but for GitHub |
17. | 7 | **Fabric REST API Python scripts** | Scripts to call Fabric APIs (for the API demo section) |
18. | 8 | **Data quality checks notebook** | Validates schema after migration |
19. | 9 | **Demo runbook** | Step-by-step instructions you can print and follow |
20. 
21. ## Data Domain: Data Center Operations
22. 
23. We use a schema that resonates with Digital Realty's business — data center monitoring:
24. - `data_centers` — facility info (name, region, capacity)
25. - `power_consumption` — energy usage telemetry
26. - `cooling_metrics` — temperature, humidity, PUE ratio
27. - `capacity_utilization` — rack utilization (added in v2)
28. - `sla_incidents` — uptime incidents (added in v2)
29. 
30. ---
31. 
32. # BEFORE THE DEMO: Setup Checklist
33. 
34. Do these steps **1–2 days before** the demo. You only do them once.
35. 
36. ## Step A: Create Fabric Workspaces
37. 
38. 1. Open your browser → go to **https://app.fabric.microsoft.com**
39. 2. Sign in with your Microsoft account
40. 3. In the left sidebar, click **Workspaces**
41. 4. Click **+ New workspace**
42.    - Name: `DigitalRealty-Dev`
43.    - Click **Apply**
44. 5. Repeat to create `DigitalRealty-UAT`
45. 6. Repeat to create `DigitalRealty-Prod`
46. 7. In each workspace, click **+ New item** → **Lakehouse**
47.    - Name: `DataCenterLakehouse`
48.    - Click **Create**
49. 
50. **What you should see:** 3 workspaces, each with an empty Lakehouse.
```

</details>

### ✅ `view`

C:\Users\sautalwar\.copilot\session-state\d91b6efe-a7c4-4a6c-a442-2561ba6f7b2c\plan.md (lines 50-120)

<details>
<summary>71 lines</summary>

```
50. **What you should see:** 3 workspaces, each with an empty Lakehouse.
51. 
52. ## Step B: Create a Fabric Deployment Pipeline
53. 
54. 1. In Fabric portal, click the **gear icon** (bottom-left) → **Deployment pipelines**
55. 2. Click **+ New pipeline**
56.    - Name: `DigitalRealty-Pipeline`
57. 3. You'll see 3 empty stages: **Development**, **Test**, **Production**
58. 4. Click **Assign a workspace** under Development → select `DigitalRealty-Dev`
59. 5. Click **Assign a workspace** under Test → select `DigitalRealty-UAT`
60. 6. Click **Assign a workspace** under Production → select `DigitalRealty-Prod`
61. 
62. **What you should see:** A pipeline with 3 stages, each linked to a workspace.
63. 
64. ## Step C: Create Azure DevOps Repo
65. 
66. 1. Go to **https://dev.azure.com**
67. 2. Create a new project: `Fabric-CICD-Demo`
68. 3. Go to **Repos** → your default repo
69. 4. Push the demo files we build (see Implementation section) to this repo
70. 
71. ## Step D: Create GitHub Repo
72. 
73. 1. Go to **https://github.com**
74. 2. Create a new repo: `fabric-cicd-demo`
75. 3. Push the same demo files to this repo
76. 
77. ## Step E: Register a Service Principal (for CI/CD automation)
78. 
79. 1. Go to **https://portal.azure.com** → **Microsoft Entra ID** → **App registrations**
80. 2. Click **+ New registration**
81.    - Name: `Fabric-CICD-SP`
82.    - Click **Register**
83. 3. Copy the **Application (client) ID** and **Tenant ID** — you'll need these
84. 4. Go to **Certificates & secrets** → **+ New client secret** → copy the secret value
85. 5. In Fabric Admin portal: **Admin portal** → **Tenant settings** → enable **Service principals can use Fabric APIs**
86. 6. In each workspace: **Settings** → **Access** → Add the Service Principal as **Contributor**
87. 
88. ## Step F: Connect Dev Workspace to Git
89. 
90. 1. Go to `DigitalRealty-Dev` workspace in Fabric
91. 2. Click **Workspace settings** (gear icon, top-right)
92. 3. Click **Git integration**
93. 4. Choose **Azure DevOps** → select your org, project, repo, branch `main`
94.    - Set the root folder to `/fabric-artifacts`
95. 5. Click **Connect and sync**
96. 
97. *(We'll show the GitHub connection live during the demo)*
98. 
99. ---
100. 
101. # DEMO SCRIPT: Exact Step-by-Step (60 minutes)
102. 
103. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
104. ## PART 1: SLIDES — Context & Problem (10 min)
105. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
106. 
107. > **Screen:** PowerPoint presentation (full screen)
108. 
109. ### Slide 1: "Microsoft Fabric SDLC Overview" (2 min)
110. **What to show:** Architecture diagram of Fabric workspaces with Git integration
111. **What to say:**
112. > "Microsoft Fabric provides a unified analytics platform. For SDLC, we use:
113. > - **Workspaces** as our environments (Dev, UAT, Prod)
114. > - **Git integration** to version-control our artifacts
115. > - **Deployment Pipelines** to promote changes across environments
116. > Let me show you how these pieces fit together."
117. 
118. ### Slide 2: "What Git Integration Tracks" (2 min)
119. **What to show:** Two-column table — ✅ tracked vs ❌ not tracked
120. **What to say:**
```

</details>

```
100. 3. Go to **Repos** → your default repo
101. 4. Push the demo files we build (see Implementation section) to this repo
102. 
103. ## Step D: Create GitHub Repo
104. 
105. 1. Go to **https://github.com**
106. 2. Create a new repo: `fabric-cicd-demo`
107. 3. Push the same demo files to this repo
108. 
109. ## Step E: Register a Service Principal (for CI/CD automation)
110. 
111. 1. Go to **https://portal.azure.com** → **Microsoft Entra ID** → **App registrations**
112. 2. Click **+ New registration**
113.    - Name: `Fabric-CICD-SP`
114.    - Click **Register**
115. 3. Copy the **Application (client) ID** and **Tenant ID** — you'll need these
116. 4. Go to **Certificates & secrets** → **+ New client secret** → copy the secret value
117. 5. In Fabric Admin portal: **Admin portal** → **Tenant settings** → enable **Service principals can use Fabric APIs**
118. 6. In each workspace: **Settings** → **Access** → Add the Service Principal as **Contributor**
119. 
120. ## Step F: Connect Dev Workspace to Git
121. 
122. 1. Go to `DigitalRealty-Dev` workspace in Fabric
123. 2. Click **Workspace settings** (gear icon, top-right)
124. 3. Click **Git integration**
125. 4. Choose **Azure DevOps** → select your org, project, repo, branch `main`
126.    - Set the root folder to `/fabric-artifacts`
127. 5. Click **Connect and sync**
128. 
129. *(We'll show the GitHub connection live during the demo)*
130. 
131. ---
132. 
133. # DEMO SCRIPT: Exact Step-by-Step (60 minutes)
134. 
135. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
136. ## PART 1: SLIDES — Context & Problem (10 min)
137. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
138. 
139. > **Screen:** PowerPoint presentation (full screen)
140. 
141. ### Slide 1: "Microsoft Fabric SDLC Overview" (2 min)
142. **What to show:** Architecture diagram of Fabric workspaces with Git integration
143. **What to say:**
144. > "Microsoft Fabric provides a unified analytics platform. For SDLC, we use:
145. > - **Workspaces** as our environments (Dev, UAT, Prod)
146. > - **Git integration** to version-control our artifacts
147. > - **Deployment Pipelines** to promote changes across environments
148. > Let me show you how these pieces fit together."
149. 
150. ### Slide 2: "What Git Integration Tracks" (2 min)
151. **What to show:** Two-column table — ✅ tracked vs ❌ not tracked
152. **What to say:**
153. > "Git integration works great for most artifacts — notebooks, data pipelines,
154. > semantic models, reports. But there's an important gap:
155. > Lakehouse table definitions, schemas, and views are NOT tracked.
156. > Only the Lakehouse container metadata is serialized."
157. 
158. ### Slide 3: "The Schema Gap" (3 min)
159. **What to show:** Diagram showing Dev Lakehouse with tables → Git (no tables) → UAT (empty)
160. **What to say:**
161. > "Here's the problem you're facing. When someone creates a table in the Dev
162. > Lakehouse — through the UI or SQL endpoint — that change stays in Dev only.
163. > Git doesn't capture it. Deployment Pipelines don't propagate it.
164. > The result is schema drift: your environments get out of sync."
165. 
166. ### Slide 4: "The Solution — Schema-as-Code" (3 min)
167. **What to show:** Solution architecture diagram
168. **What to say:**
169. > "The recommended approach is Schema-as-Code. We write migration scripts —
170. > SQL or PySpark — that define our table structures. These scripts live in Git.
171. > Our CI/CD pipeline runs these scripts against each environment.
172. > Combined with Deployment Pipelines for other artifacts, we get full coverage."
173. 
174. ### Slide 5: "Demo Roadmap" (1 min)
175. **What to show:** Numbered list of what you'll demonstrate
176. **What to say:**
177. > "Let me show you this working end-to-end. We'll walk through:
178. > 1. The environment setup
179. > 2. Standard Git CI/CD (the happy path)
180. > 3. The Lakehouse gap (the problem)
181. > 4. Schema-as-Code (the solution)
182. > 5. REST API automation
183. > Let's switch to the live environment."
184. 
185. ---
186. 
187. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
188. ## PART 2: LIVE — Environment Setup (8 min)
189. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
190. 
191. ### Step 2.1: Show the Fabric Workspaces
192. > **Screen:** Browser → https://app.fabric.microsoft.com
193. 
194. 1. Click **Workspaces** in the left sidebar
195. 2. Click on `DigitalRealty-Dev` — show the Lakehouse inside
196. 3. Go back → click on `DigitalRealty-UAT` — show it's similar
197. 4. Go back → click on `DigitalRealty-Prod` — show it's similar
198. 
199. **What to say:**
200. > "We have 3 workspaces mirroring our environments. Each has a Lakehouse
201. > called DataCenterLakehouse. Right now they're empty."
202. 
203. ### Step 2.2: Show the Deployment Pipeline
204. > **Screen:** Browser → Fabric portal → Deployment Pipelines
205. 
206. 1. Click the **gear icon** (bottom-left) → **Deployment pipelines**
207. 2. Click on `DigitalRealty-Pipeline`
208. 3. Point out the 3 stages: Development → Test → Production
209. 4. Show each stage is linked to the correct workspace
210. 
211. **What to say:**
212. > "This is Fabric's built-in Deployment Pipeline. It lets us promote artifacts
213. > from Dev to Test to Production with one click. We'll use this for
214. > notebooks, pipelines, and other standard artifacts."
215. 
216. ### Step 2.3: Show the Azure DevOps Repo
217. > **Screen:** Browser → New tab → https://dev.azure.com → Fabric-CICD-Demo → Repos
218. 
219. 1. Show the folder structure:
220.    - `/lakehouse-migrations/migrations/` — the schema scripts
221.    - `/pipelines/` — the CI/CD pipeline definitions
222.    - `/notebooks/` — supporting notebooks
223.    - `/scripts/` — REST API helper scripts
224. 2. Click into `/lakehouse-migrations/migrations/` → show the SQL and Python files
225. 
226. **What to say:**
227. > "Here's our Azure DevOps repo. Notice the lakehouse-migrations folder —
228. > this is where we store our schema-as-code. Each version is a separate file."
229. 
230. ### Step 2.4: Show the GitHub Repo
231. > **Screen:** Browser → New tab → https://github.com → fabric-cicd-demo
232. 
233. 1. Show the same folder structure in GitHub
234. 2. Point out it mirrors the Azure DevOps repo
235. 
236. **What to say:**
237. > "We also have this in GitHub. You can use either platform — or both."
238. 
239. ### Step 2.5: Show Git Integration in Dev Workspace
240. > **Screen:** Browser → Fabric portal → DigitalRealty-Dev workspace
241. 
242. 1. Click **Workspace settings** (gear icon, top-right)
243. 2. Click **Git integration** in the left menu
244. 3. Show the connection to Azure DevOps (already configured in setup)
245. 4. Point out: Connected org, project, repo, branch, root folder
246. 
247. **What to say:**
248. > "The Dev workspace is connected to our Azure DevOps repo. When we make
249. > changes here, we can commit them to Git. And vice versa."
250.
```

<sub>⏱️ 2336m 5s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\docs\demo_runbook.md.**

C:\Users\sautalwar\Downloads\repos\Fabric_GitHub\docs\demo_runbook.md

<details>
<summary>167 lines</summary>

```
1. # Demo Runbook — Fabric CI/CD for Lakehouse Schema Evolution
2. > **Duration:** 60 minutes | **Audience:** Digital Realty engineering team  
3. > **Presenter:** [Your Name] | **Date:** [Date]
4. 
5. ## Quick URLs
6. - [ ] **Navigate** to Fabric portal — https://app.fabric.microsoft.com/home?experience=data-engineering
7. - [ ] **Navigate** to Deployment pipelines — https://app.fabric.microsoft.com/groups/me/deploymentpipelines?experience=data-engineering
8. - [ ] **Navigate** to Azure DevOps repo — https://dev.azure.com/<org>/<project>/_git/<repo>
9. - [ ] **Navigate** to Azure DevOps pipeline — https://dev.azure.com/<org>/<project>/_build?definitionId=<pipeline-id>
10. - [ ] **Navigate** to GitHub repo — https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>
11. - [ ] **Navigate** to GitHub Actions — https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/actions
12. - [ ] **Navigate** to Fabric REST API docs — https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/fabric/
13. - [ ] **Navigate** to Fabric Git automation docs — https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/git-integration/git-automation
14. - [ ] **Navigate** to Fabric deployment pipeline automation docs — https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/deployment-pipelines/pipeline-automation-fabric
15. 
16. ## Pre-Demo Checklist
17. - [ ] 3 Fabric workspaces created (`DigitalRealty-Dev`, `DigitalRealty-UAT`, `DigitalRealty-Prod`)
18. - [ ] Deployment Pipeline configured and mapped Dev → UAT → Prod
19. - [ ] Azure DevOps repo with demo files pushed
20. - [ ] GitHub repo with demo files pushed
21. - [ ] Dev workspace connected to Git
22. - [ ] `v001_baseline_tables` already applied in all 3 environments
23. - [ ] `v002_capacity_and_sustainability` ready to show in Git
24. - [ ] Service Principal registered and configured; client secret hidden

31. ## Part 1: Slides (10 min)
32. ### 0:00-2:00 — Slide 1: SDLC Overview
33. - [ ] **Show** Slide 1 and frame the Dev → UAT → Prod flow.
34.   > "This is the SDLC pattern our teams already use; Fabric needs to fit into it cleanly."
35. ### 2:00-4:00 — Slide 2: What Git Tracks

37.   > "Fabric Git integration covers most artifacts well, so the repo becomes the source of truth."
38. ### 4:00-7:00 — Slide 3: The Schema Gap
39. - [ ] **Say** that Lakehouse schema changes are the missing piece in normal Fabric promotion.
40.   > "The gap is Lakehouse schema evolution—new tables and altered schemas do not ride along automatically."
41. ### 7:00-10:00 — Slides 4-5: Solution and Roadmap
42. - [ ] **Show** Slide 4 with schema-as-code: versioned SQL/PySpark migrations plus a runner notebook.
43.   > "We treat schema like application code: version it, review it, promote it, and re-run it safely."
44. - [ ] **Show** Slide 5 with the demo roadmap: Dev change, CI/CD, GitHub parity, REST APIs.
45.   > "Now I’ll walk the full path end to end."
46.

254. ## PART 3: LIVE — Standard CI/CD Flow (8 min)
255. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
256. 
257. > **Goal:** Show that non-Lakehouse artifacts flow perfectly through Git.
258. 
259. ### Step 3.1: Create a Notebook in Dev

267.    print("Running quality checks...")
268.    ```

270.

273.

276.

278. 2. Click **Source control**
279. 3. You'll see the notebook listed as a pending change
280. 4. Type a commit message: `Add DataQualityChecks notebook`
281. 5. Click **Commit**
282. 
283. **What to say:**

320. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
321.

323. 
324. ### Step 4.1: Create Tables in Dev Lakehouse
325. > **Screen:** Browser → Fabric portal → DigitalRealty-Dev → DataCenterLakehouse
326. 
327. 1. Click on the Lakehouse → click **SQL analytics endpoint** (top-right dropdown, switch from "Lakehouse" to "SQL analytics endpoint")
328. 2. You're now in the SQL query editor
329. 3. Paste and run this SQL:
330.    ```sql
331.    CREATE TABLE data_centers (
332.        dc_id           STRING,
333.        dc_name         STRING,
334.        region          STRING,
335.        city            STRING,
336.        total_capacity_kw DOUBLE
337.    );
338.    ```
339. 4. Click **Run** (▶ button)

341. 6. **Pause here** — let the audience see the table exists
342.

373.

410. ### Step 5.2: Show the Migration Runner Notebook (3 min)
411. > **Screen:** Browser → Azure DevOps → Repos → lakehouse-migrations/migration_runner.py
412. 
413. 1. Walk through the code:
414.    - **Parameters:** `target_environment` (Dev/UAT/Prod), `migration_type` (sql/pyspark)
415.    - **Step 1:** Reads the list of available migration files
416.    - **Step 2:** Checks which migrations have already been applied (reads state from a Delta table)
417.    - **Step 3:** Runs only the NEW migrations that haven't been applied
418.    - **Step 4:** Records each successful migration in the state table
419. 
420. **What to say:**
421. > "This notebook is the engine. It's parameterized — you tell it which
422. > environment to target and whether to use SQL or PySpark. It's idempotent —
423. > you can run it 10 times and it won't break anything. It only applies
424. > new migrations."
425.

432.    - `migration_type` = `sql`
433. 3. Click **Run all** (▶▶ button)
434. 4. Watch the cells execute — they'll print status messages like:

````
500. 
501. ### Step 6.1: Show the API Helper Script (3 min)
502. > **Screen:** Browser → Azure DevOps → Repos → scripts/fabric_api_helper.py
503. 
504. 1. Walk through the Python script:
505.    - **Authentication:** Get OAuth token using Service Principal credentials
506.    - **list_workspaces()** — Lists all workspaces
507.    - **list_items()** — Lists items in a workspace
508.    - **trigger_notebook()** — Triggers a notebook run via Job Scheduler API
509.    - **get_job_status()** — Polls for notebook completion
510.    - **deploy_pipeline()** — Triggers Deployment Pipeline via API
511.    - **git_commit()** — Commits workspace changes to Git programmatically
512. 
513. ### Step 6.2: Run API Calls Live (5 min)
514. > **Screen:** Browser → Fabric portal → Open a Notebook -OR- use Postman
515. 
516. **Option A: From a Fabric Notebook** (easier for the audience to follow)
517. 
518. 1. Open a new notebook in the Dev workspace
519. 2. Run pre-prepared cells that call the APIs:
520. 
521.    **Cell 1: Authenticate**
522.    ```python
523.    import requests
524.    token = mssparkutils.credentials.getToken("https://api.fabric.microsoft.com")
525.    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
526.    ```
527. 
528.    **Cell 2: List Workspaces**
529.    ```python
530.    r = requests.get("https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces", headers=headers)
531.    for ws in r.json()["value"]:
532.        print(f"  {ws['displayName']} ({ws['id']})")
533.    ```
534. 
535.    **Cell 3: List Items in UAT Workspace**
536.    ```python
537.    r = requests.get(f"https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{UAT_WS_ID}/items", headers=headers)
538.    for item in r.json()["value"]:
539.        print(f"  {item['type']}: {item['displayName']}")
540.    ```
541. 
542.    **Cell 4: Trigger Notebook Run**
543.    ```python
544.    r = requests.post(
545.        f"https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{UAT_WS_ID}/items/{NOTEBOOK_ID}/jobs/instances?jobType=RunNotebook",
546.        headers=headers,
547.        json={"executionData": {"parameters": {"target_environment": "UAT"}}}
548.    )
549.    print(f"Job triggered! Location: {r.headers.get('Location')}")
550.    ```
551. 
552. **What to say:**
553. > "Everything we showed through the UI and pipelines — it's all available
554. > via REST APIs. You can build any custom automation on top of this.
555. > This is how the CI/CD pipeline works under the hood."
556. 
557. ---
558. 
559. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
560. ## WRAP-UP (3 min)
561. ## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
562. 
563. > **Screen:** Switch back to PowerPoint → Slide 4 (Solution Architecture)
564. 
565. **What to say:**
566. > "Let me summarize what we've shown:
567. >
568. > 1. **Standard artifacts** (notebooks, pipelines, reports) → Git + Deployment Pipelines work perfectly.
569. > 2. **Lakehouse schemas** → Not tracked by Git today. The workaround is Schema-as-Code.
570. > 3. **Schema-as-Code** → Version-controlled migration scripts (SQL or PySpark), run by a migration notebook, triggered automatically by CI/CD.
571. > 4. **Both Azure DevOps and GitHub** work for this.
572. > 5. **Fabric REST APIs** power all the automation.
573. >
574. > One more important note: **Fabric Warehouse** DOES support object-level
575. > Git integration. If you need deep schema versioning natively, consider
576. > using Warehouse for structured tables and Lakehouse for unstructured data.
577. >
578. > Questions?"
579. 
580. ---
````

</details>

Let me think about what hard questions could come up:

**Part 1 (Slides):**
- What exactly IS a Lakehouse? How is it different from a Data Warehouse?


- Why can't Fabric just track Lakehouse schemas like it does for Warehouse?
- What is Delta Lake and how does it relate to Lakehouse?
- Can we use Warehouse instead? What are the trade-offs?

**Part 2 (Environment Tour):**
- Why 3 separate workspaces vs 1 workspace with branching?
- What's the difference between Deployment Pipelines and Git-based CI/CD?
- Can we use Fabric capacities to isolate environments?
- What about data isolation between environments?

**Part 3 (Happy Path):** I'm continuing to map out the tougher questions that might surface during the demo, particularly around environment strategy, deployment approaches, and whether alternative solutions could work better for their use case.

**Part 4 (The Gap):** Now I'm digging into the core limitation—why Lakehouse schema tracking falls short compared to Warehouse, what actually gets serialized in Git, and how schema drift and untracked UI changes could become operational headaches.

**Part 5 (Schema-as-Code):** I'm thinking through the practical implementation challenges like handling rollbacks, managing data migrations alongside schema changes, resolving conflicts, and whether the approach differs between Lakehouse and Warehouse.

**Part 6 (REST APIs):** Moving to the API layer—rate limits, secure authentication, managing long-running operations, and whether we can expose migration status monitoring.

Now I'm realizing I should build out a knowledge base section in the demo guide with Fabric concept explainers, then add Q&A cards throughout each part with detailed answers, plus a toggle to show or hide them. This is a bigger refactor, so I'll approach it systematically.*

{"type":"text","text":{"value":"Here's a detailed comparison of Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse vs Warehouse, with a focus on differences, Git integration, and schema tracking as relevant to 2025 and 2026:\n\n### Core Differences: Fabric Lakehouse vs Warehouse\n\n#### 1. **Underlying Architecture & Data Types**\n- **Lakehouse**: \n  - Stores data as files (Delta Lake format) in OneLake, supporting structured, semi-structured (e.g., JSON, XML), and unstructured data (images, logs, text).\n  - Optimized for data engineering, machine learning, and big data transformations using Apache Spark.\n  - Designed to be accessed via notebooks (Python, Scala, Spark SQL) and read-only SQL endpoints.\n  - Schema is flexible (schema-on-read and schema evolution supported)【3:3†source】【3:4†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n- **Warehouse**: \n  - Built as a traditional SQL database, storing structured data in database pages, with full T-SQL support for both read and write operations.\n  - Best for business intelligence, reporting, and scenarios requiring strong transactional consistency (multi-table transactions, ACID compliance).\n  - Optimized for high-throughput, low-latency queries, supporting indexes, constraints, and cross-database queries【3:3†source】【3:5†source】【3:4†source】.\n\n#### 2. **Typical Users and Workflows**\n- **Lakehouse**: \n  - Data engineers and scientists who need to land, transform, and experiment with messy/raw data.\n  - Suited for Spark-first workflows, big data processing, and experimental pipelines.\n  - Useful for medallion architecture (bronze, silver, gold layers)【3:3†source】【3:6†source】.\n- **Warehouse**: \n  - BI developers, analysts, and teams who work primarily in SQL and require governed, stable datasets for reporting and metric delivery.\n  - Better for highly curated, production-ready data models【3:6†source】.\n\n#### 3. **Integration Modes**\n- **Lakehouse**: \n  - Supports Power BI Direct Lake (direct access to storage) – only available with Lakehouse.\n  - SQL endpoint for read-only analytics; data written via Spark, Dataflows, or pipelines.\n- **Warehouse**: \n  - Supports DirectQuery and Import modes in Power BI.\n  - Full support for SQL-based CRUD operations【3:4†source】.\n\n### Git Integration (and Schema Tracking)\n\n#### **Git Integration**\n- **Lakehouse & Warehouse**: Both have increasing support for Git integration (commonly Azure DevOps and GitHub) as part of the Fabric platform's push toward source-controlled, CI/CD-friendly analytics environments. \n  - **Key features** (recent and upcoming for 2025/2026): \n    - Track versioned changes to notebooks, data pipelines, SQL scripts, and, increasingly, schema definitions and transformations.\n    - Push/pull data models and pipeline configurations, with increased DevOps automation on the roadmap【3:2†source】.\n  - **Warehouse-specific**: More granular Git integration for T-SQL objects (such as tables, views, procedures). SQL endpoints in Lakehouse are read-only, so changes to data or schema are handled via the underlying files/notebooks, requiring a different DevOps workflow.\n\n#### **Schema Tracking**\n- **Lakehouse**: \n  - Schema evolution and versioning are a native feature through Delta Lake (\"time travel\", change history, schema evolution).\n  - Developers can track changes in both the structure and data over time – beneficial for auditing and reverting to previous schema states.\n  - Schema information is accessible via SQL endpoint (for analysis) but modifications typically go via Spark/notebooks【3:3†source】【3:4†source】.\n- **Warehouse**: \n  - Traditional database schema management – changes are tracked, can be integrated into CI/CD with Git.\n  - Advanced schema compare and migration tools available within the SQL ecosystem.\n  - Full DML and DDL support for managing schema through T-SQL, tracked via code repositories as needed.\n\n### Future Outlook (2025–2026)\n\n- Both Lakehouse and Warehouse are expected to deepen integration with DevOps and data governance, especially with automated schema tracking, multi-environment pipeline deployment, and integrated testing.\n- Microsoft's roadmap includes expanding artifact-level Git integration, automated lineage, and built-in schema drift detection across both paradigms but especially for Lakehouse Delta tables and curated dataflows【3:2†source】.\n\n---\n\n**Bottom Line:**  \n- Use **Lakehouse** for flexible data engineering, big data environments, mixed data types, and advanced schema evolution/versioning.\n- Use **Warehouse** when you require classic SQL experience, strong transactional support, and curated, consistent reporting for the business.\n- Both support modern Git integration and schema tracking, but with different workflows and depth; expect more seamless DevOps features across Fabric Lakehouse and Warehouse by 2025–2026【3:2†source】【3:3†source】【3:4†source】【3:6†source】【3:5†source】.","annotations":[{"text":"【3:3†source】","start_index":720,"end_index":732,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse vs. Warehouse in Microsoft Fabric: What’s the Difference?","url":"https://www.daymarksi.com/cole-tramps-microsoft-insights/lakehouse-vs.-warehouse-in-microsoft-fabric-whats-the-difference"}},{"text":"【3:4†source】","start_index":732,"end_index":744,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse **Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse schema evolution best practices Delta Lake tables CI/CD limitations**

````
{"type":"text","text":{"value":"Here’s a comprehensive guide on schema evolution best practices for Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse using Delta Lake tables, with emphasis on CI/CD pipeline limitations and considerations:\n\n---\n## Schema Evolution in Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse\n\n### What is Schema Evolution?\nSchema evolution is the ability to change a table’s schema—such as adding, removing, or modifying columns—over time while maintaining backward compatibility and data integrity. This is crucial in agile data environments where incoming data structures can shift as business requirements evolve.\n\n### Delta Lake Tables—Why They Matter in Fabric\nMicrosoft Fabric Lakehouse uses Delta Lake as the default storage layer, which brings ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and efficient batch/stream processing. Delta Lake's schema evolution capabilities make it easier to adapt tables to new data without breaking downstream processes or corrupting historical data. These features are especially useful when Data Engineering pipelines are versioned and deployed via CI/CD tools【3:5†source】【3:2†source】【3:9†source】.\n\n---\n\n## Best Practices for Schema Evolution in Delta Lake on Microsoft Fabric\n\n1. **Enable Schema Evolution Selectively:**\n   - Delta Lake enforces schemas by default. To allow evolution (adding/removing columns), you must explicitly set `mergeSchema` (during data writes) or enable `autoMerge`.\n   - Example:\n     ```python\n     df.write.option(\"mergeSchema\", \"true\").mode(\"append\").format(\"delta\").save(table_path)\n     ```\n   - For ongoing changes, set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` globally, but be cautious—it can inadvertently introduce undesired schema changes if your data sources are not tightly controlled【3:6†source】【3:8†source】.\n\n2. **Versioning and Backward Compatibility:**\n   - Track Delta table versions and metadata in source control or governance tools. Each schema change/version should be documented or tagged, ideally via data cataloging.\n   - Use Delta Lake’s time travel to inspect or rollback to previous schema versions if needed.\n\n3. **Column Additions vs. Modifications:**\n   - Adding new columns is supported and existing records will show null values for them.\n   - Modifying (e.g., changing type) or renaming columns should be managed with caution. For some types (e.g., increasing precision), Delta can upcast data types; for others, you might need to rewrite data (using `overwriteSchema` option)【3:8†source】.\n\n4. **Careful Management of Downstream Dependencies:**\n   - Changing or moving tables between schemas requires updating all references in notebooks, dataflows, and Power BI datasets, as names will change (e.g., `schema.table`).\n   - Broken dependencies are a common source of failures when evolving schemas in a shared data environment【3:0†source】【3:4†source】.\n\n5. **Consistent Use of Schemas and Naming:**\n   - Use schemas to logically group tables (e.g., by domain, department) and simplify access control.\n   - Maintain a clear, documented approach to naming and organizing tables and schemas for discoverability and governance in Fabric’s Lakehouse Explorer【3:0†source】【3:4†source】.\n\n---\n\n## CI/CD Limitations and Considerations\n\n### Current CI/CD Gaps in Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse:\n\n- **Schema and Data Migration Not Fully Declarative**: There is no built-in migration tool for evolving existing Delta tables across environments. You need to script schema changes and version management manually as deployable assets in your pipeline (e.g., notebook scripts, T-SQL files).\n- **Cross-Environment Consistency**: CI/CD is robust for code and configuration, but synchronizing Delta table schemas and data across dev, test, and prod requires additional tracking/automation.\n- **Table Evolution in Git-integrated Deployments**: Changes to table schemas may not be automatically detected or applied between workspaces during pipeline deployments. Manual intervention/scripts may be necessary to avoid deployment drift.\n- **No “Rollback” Mechanism at Deployment Layer**: You must manage Delta’s versioning manually or via custom scripts in your CI/CD tooling (e.g., deploy, run migration notebook, validate, fallback if errors by switching Delta “version”)【3:3†source】【3:6†source】.\n\n### Recommendations:\n\n- **Automate Schema Evolution Scripts**: Store notebook or SQL scripts for table changes in source control and run them as part of the deployment process.\n- **Validate Upgrades with Data Quality Checks**: After running schema evolution scripts, validate the new shape of data and downstream reports.\n- **Tag and Document Data Releases**: Use Delta version/history metadata, workspace documentation, or external catalogs to record changes.\n- **Monitor Feature Updates**: Fabric is evolving rapidly—new migration, deployment, or rollback features for data pipelines and Lakehouse tables may become available. Monitor official docs and Fabric release notes【3:4†source】.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| Practice                      | Description                                                                                    | Limitation/Warning                             |\n|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|\n| Use `mergeSchema` or `autoMerge` | Enables schema evolution for new columns, must be explicitly set                        | Risk of unplanned schema drift if not managed |\n| Document each schema version   | Track via Delta logs and/or external catalog                                                  | No built-in Git-like diff for table schemas   |\n| Upgrade through scripts       | Include schema migrations in code repositories; automate in CI/CD                             | Manual management required                    |\n| Validate downstream updates   | Update all references to table/schema names and test downstream dependencies                   | Manual effort, can break reports/notebooks    |\n| Monitor Fabric enhancements   | Check for evolving tooling for migration and schema management                                 | Feature set rapidly changing                  |\n\n---\n\n**References**:  \n- Microsoft Docs: Lakehouse Schemas【3:0†source】, Delta Lake Tables【3:1†source】【3:3†source】\n- DeepWiki on Lakehouse & Delta Tables【3:2†source】\n- Delta Lake official blog—Schema Evolution【3:6†source】\n- Seequality—Delta Lake Schema Evolution and Enforcement【3:8†source】\n- MSSQLTips on Schema Management in Fabric Lakehouse【3:4†source】","annotations":[{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":1056,"end_index":1068,"url_citation":{"title":"Optimizing Your Data Architecture in Fabric with Delta ### ✅ `web_search`

**Microsoft Fabric Git integration what artifacts are serialized tracked 2025 limitations Lakehouse metadata**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Microsoft Fabric Git integration what artifacts are serialized tracked 2025 limitations Lakehouse metadata"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"text","text":{"value":"Microsoft Fabric's Git integration for Lakehouse supports versioning and deployment management, but its capabilities and limitations—especially concerning serialized and tracked artifacts—are important to understand for 2025 and beyond.\n\n### What Artifacts Are Serialized & Tracked?\n\n**Lakehouse Git integration** in Fabric, as of 2025, serializes and tracks only the **Lakehouse container artifact metadata**. This means:\n- **Lakehouse Metadata (display name, description, logical GUID)**: Yes, tracked.\n- **OneLake Shortcuts Metadata**: Yes, tracked.\n- **OneLake Security Data Access Roles (DAR) Metadata**: Yes, tracked (as a preview feature).\n- **Tables (Delta & non-Delta)**: No, not tracked.\n- **Spark Views**: No, not tracked.\n- **Folders in Files Section**: No, not tracked.\n\nOnly metadata is stored; the **underlying data (tables/files)** is never versioned or overwritten via Git or deployment pipelines. The actual metadata tracked is documented in more detail here: lakehouse metadata, OneLake/External/Internal Shortcuts, security roles, and container info are serialized to Git (typically as JSON)【3:0†source】【3:6†source】.\n\n### 2025 Limitations\n\n- **No Table or View Definition Tracking:** The structure, schema, or DDL of tables and views in the Lakehouse are *not* serialized or versioned via Git. Only the existence/config/metadata of the Lakehouse “container” is. You can't roll back or promote changes to Lakehouse tables or views via Fabric's Git integration directly.\n- **Data Is Never Overwritten:** Git/deployment actions never affect the actual tabular data or files in storage, reducing the risk of accidental data loss but limiting full reproducibility.\n- **Manual Processes Required for Table/View CI/CD:** If you need to track, deploy, or synchronize the schema of tables/views (for example, for DevOps-style CI/CD), you have to manually script the extraction (via notebooks/scripts querying metadata or Information Schema views) and deploy to target environments【3:5†source】.\n- **Object Type Flexibility:** You can choose which object types to track in some cases, but the main tracking is limited to GA (general availability) artifacts, which do not include table/view definitions【3:0†source】.\n- **Large-Scale Solutions:** For large solutions (hundreds of tables/views), this limitation is significant; scripting or automation (outside Fabric's native Git integration) is necessary.\n- **Other Git Limitations:** Commit size limits (e.g., 25 MB for Azure DevOps with Service Principal, 50 MB for GitHub), no support for submodules, sovereign clouds, or certain identity configurations【3:8†source】.\n\n### Community Workarounds\n\n- **Manual Notebooks:** Manually script table/view definitions and deploy them as notebooks.\n- **Dynamic Notebooks:** Write notebooks that enumerate and export all table/view definitions programmatically—though maintaining and deploying this is still outside the native Git integration.\n- **External CI/CD (e.g., Azure DevOps Pipelines):** Orchestrate schema extraction and deployment via external pipelines for more dynamic and less manual processes【3:5†source】.\n\n### Summary Table\n\n| Artifact/Metadata             | Serialized/Tracked in Git?        | Notes                        |\n|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|\n| Lakehouse metadata (container)| Yes                               | Display name, GUID, etc.     |\n| Shortcut metadata             | Yes                               | OneLake, external/internal   |\n| Security data access roles    | Yes (Preview)                     | Data-access-roles.json       |\n| Table/View schema, DDL        | No                                | Must script manually         |\n| Data files/tables/folders     | No                                | Never overwritten/tracked    |\n\n### References\n- Official documentation and community reports make it clear that only container-level and select metadata are tracked, not object definitions like tables or views—which is a critical limitation for enterprise CI/CD needs【3:0†source】【3:5†source】【3:6†source】.\n\nIf you need full object-level tracking/serialization (schema or DDL), you'll need custom or external solutions for now. Microsoft signals ongoing improvements, so it’s worth watching for updates in future Fabric releases.\n\n---\n**Further Reading:**\n- [Microsoft Fabric Docs: Lakehouse Git Integration and Deployment Pipelines](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-git-deployment-pipelines)【3:0†source】\n- [Community Discussion on Lakehouse Table/View Deployment](https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Best-Practice-for-Lakehouse-Table-amp-View-Deployments/m-p/4714052)【3:5†source】","annotations":[{"text":"【3:0†source】","start_index":1111,"end_index":1123,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse git integration and deployment pipelines - Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-git-deployment-pipelines"}},{"text":"【3:6†source】","start_index":1123,"end_index":1135,"url_citation":{"title":"Fabric Lakehouse: What metadata gets synced to Git repository?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Fabric-Lakehouse-What-metadata-gets-synced-to-Git-repository/m-p/3944370"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":1991,"end_index":2003,"url_citation":{"title":"Best Practice for Lakehouse Table & View Deployments?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Best-Practice-for-Lakehouse-Table-amp-View-Deployments/m-p/4714052"}},{"text":"【3:0†source】","start_index":2210,"end_index":2222,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse git integration and deployment pipelines - Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-git-deployment-pipelines"}},{"text":"【3:8†source】","start_index":2613,"end_index":2625,"url_citation":{"title":"fabric-docs/docs/cicd/includes/git-limitations.md at main ...","url":"https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/fabric-docs/blob/main/docs/cicd/includes/git-limitations.md"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":3105,"end_index":3117,"url_citation":{"title":"Best Practice for Lakehouse Table & View Deployments?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Best-Practice-for-Lakehouse-Table-amp-View-Deployments/m-p/4714052"}},{"text":"【3:0†source】","start_index":4083,"end_index":4095,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse git integration and deployment pipelines - Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-git-deployment-pipelines"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":4095,"end_index":4107,"url_citation":{"title":"Best Practice for Lakehouse Table & View Deployments?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Best-Practice-for-Lakehouse-Table-amp-View-Deployments/m-p/4714052"}},{"text":"【3:6†source】","start_index":4107,"end_index":4119,"url_citation":{"title":"Fabric Lakehouse: What metadata gets synced to Git repository?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Fabric-Lakehouse-What-metadata-gets-synced-to-Git-repository/m-p/3944370"}},{"text":"【3:0†source】","start_index":4541,"end_index":4553,"url_citation":{"title":"Lakehouse git integration and deployment pipelines - Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-git-deployment-pipelines"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":4740,"end_index":4752,"url_citation":{"title":"Best Practice for Lakehouse Table & View Deployments?","url":"https://community.fabric.microsoft.com/t5/Data-Engineering/Best-Practice-for-Lakehouse-Table-amp-View-Deployments/m-p/4714052"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"Microsoft Fabric Git integration what artifacts are serialized tracked 2025 limitations Lakehouse metadata","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=Microsoft+Fabric+Git+integration+what+artifacts+are+serialized+tracked+2025+limitations+Lakehouse+metadata"}],"annotations":null}

**Microsoft Fabric deployment pipelines vs Git CI/CD comparison best practices enterprise schema drift detection**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>
