```json
{
  "query": "Microsoft Fabric deployment pipelines vs Git CI/CD comparison best practices enterprise schema drift detection"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"text","text":{"value":"When planning Microsoft Fabric CI/CD strategies for enterprise-grade deployments, it's crucial to compare two primary approaches: native Microsoft Fabric Deployment Pipelines and Git-based CI/CD (using Azure DevOps or GitHub). Both have their strengths and distinct use cases, especially regarding schema drift detection and overall best practices.\n\n### 1. **Microsoft Fabric Deployment Pipelines**\n**Suitable for:** Premium (F64+) environments.\n\n**Key features:**\n- Visual, no/low-code experience for promoting artifacts (notebooks, reports, models) across Dev, Test, and Prod workspaces.\n- Environment-specific configuration (e.g., dataset connection strings, rules to exclude sensitive data).\n- Manual or scheduled progression through pipeline stages; supports approvals and validations.\n- Good for teams preferring a graphical interface and less code-centric processes.\n\n**Enterprise schema drift detection:**\n- Deployment Pipelines do not provide built-in schema drift detection. Schema drift (when source/target diverge due to outside changes) must be managed through manual artifact comparisons or external scripts.\n- For SQL-specific deployments (Tables, Procs, etc), only “shell” deployments are supported—data and objects require DACPAC or manual scripts for full drift management. The pipeline's configuration rules can help, but aren't as robust as full Git-based diffing【3:7†source】.\n\n**Best practices:**\n- Use parameter rules to control environment variables (connection strings, secrets, etc) as artifacts move between environments.\n- Regularly audit workspace contents to avoid “drift” from manual/direct edits in downstream environments.\n- For granular drift detection, supplement with artifact-level monitoring or use Git integration in parallel for snapshot diffing【3:4†source】【3:3†source】.\n\n---\n\n### 2. **Git-based CI/CD (Azure DevOps/GitHub Actions)**\n**Suitable for:** All Fabric SKUs, especially where Premium is not available or complex workflows required; essential for full Source of Truth (SOT) governance.\n\n**Key features:**\n- All changes managed in Git; each workspace/environment mapped to a branch.\n- Automated build/release pipelines process updates, run tests, handle approvals, and deploy artifacts to target workspaces.\n- Allows integration with external tooling for advanced validation, transformations, and reporting.\n- Supports full history, rollback, and robust branch-based workflows (feature, main, release, etc).\n\n**Enterprise schema drift detection:**\n- Git is the definitive source—every change is tracked, reviewed (pull requests), and can be diffed on commit.\n- Drift between source and target can be detected via Git diff and automated checks in CI pipelines.\n- For data/schema objects, supplement with tools like DACPAC for SQL projects to handle DDL and ensure deployment doesn't unintentionally overwrite or lose data【3:7†source】【3:4†source】.\n\n**Best practices:**\n- Clearly define branch strategy (main, develop, feature, release).\n- Require pull requests with code review/approvals for any changes promoted to Test/Prod.\n- Use “Build” pipeline to validate artifacts, run unit tests, and scan for policy violations before deployments.\n- Automate drift detection: e.g., nightly pipeline that compares deployed workspace against Git; alert on deviations【3:9†source】【3:5†source】【3:6†source】.\n- Use IaC (Infrastructure as Code) scripts for Data Engineering artifacts, DACPAC for SQL, and ensure test coverage prior to merges.\n\n---\n\n### 3. **Hybrid & Additional Considerations**\nEnterprises often adopt a **hybrid** approach: use Git as the Source of Truth and Fabric Pipelines for visual, staged promotion, combining governance with ease of use. Key points:\n- Always avoid manual changes in non-Dev environments.\n- Build-in pipeline steps for drift detection (e.g., using PowerShell/Python scripts to compare workspace artifacts to Git).\n- Automate notifications for policy violations, drift, or unauthorized direct edits.\n\n---\n\n### **Summary Table**\n\n| Feature/Goal          | Fabric Deployment Pipeline         | Git-based CI/CD (Azure DevOps/GitHub)      |\n|----------------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|\n| Visual workflow      | Yes                                | Limited (via pipeline dashboards)          |\n| Code-centric         | No (mostly point-and-click)        | Yes (YAML pipelines, scripts)              |\n| Environment config   | Strong (parameter rules)           | Strong (template/script-driven)            |\n| Schema drift detection| Manual/limited                    | Automated/robust via Git diff              |\n| Backup/rollback      | Snapshot history per deployment    | Full versioning, history, rollback         |\n| Best for…            | Simplicity, small-medium teams     | Complexity, auditability, enterprise scale |\n\n---\n\n### **References**\n- Detailed workflow comparison, setup, and architectural samples from Formula5 and DeepWiki【3:0†source】【3:4†source】.\n- Microsoft official doc “CI/CD workflow options in Fabric”【3:2†source】.\n- Best practices and architectural guidance from Microsoft Fabric Catalyst【3:5†source】.\n- Real-world CI/CD challenges and drift detection, Twyzer and GitHub discussions【3:6†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n---\n\n**Conclusion:**  \nFor enterprises, the preferred CI/CD strategy involves using Git-based workflows as the single source of truth, automating all deployments via pipelines, and supplementing with Fabric Deployment Pipelines for streamlined stage promotion and environment config. Schema drift should always be detected using automated artifacts diffing between workspaces and Git, with additional tooling for DDL (like DACPAC) where structure and data integrity is critical. Regular reviews, audit trails, and least-privileged access models round out best practices for robust, scalable deployments.","annotations":[{"text":"【3:7†source】","start_index":1383,"end_index":1395,"url_citation":{"title":"Clarification on Choosing Fabric CI/CD vs. Deployment Pipelines for ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/fabric-cicd/issues/484"}},{"text":"【3:4†source】","start_index":1784,"end_index":1796,"url_citation":{"title":"CI/CD Solutions | microsoft/fabric-toolbox | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/microsoft/fabric-toolbox/5.2-cicd-solutions"}},{"text":"【3:3†source】","start_index":1796,"end_index":1808,"url_citation":{"title":"Fabric CI/CD with Git deployment and release strategies","url":"https://data-marc.com/2024/07/09/fabric-ci-cd-with-git-deployment-and-release-strategies/"}},{"text":"【3:7†source】","start_index":2867,"end_index":2879,"url_citation":{"title":"Clarification on Choosing Fabric CI/CD vs. Deployment Pipelines for ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/fabric-cicd/issues/484"}},{"text":"【3:4†source】","start_index":2879,"end_index":2891,"url_citation":{"title":"CI/CD Solutions | microsoft/fabric-toolbox | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/microsoft/fabric-toolbox/5.2-cicd-solutions"}},{"text":"【3:9†source】","start_index":3301,"end_index":3313,"url_citation":{"title":"Git Integration for Version Control and CI/CD Workflows in Microsoft ...","url":"https://cs-worldwide.com/git-integration-for-version-control-and-ci-cd-workflows-in-microsoft-fabric-the-direct-lake-approach/"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":3313,"end_index":3325,"url_citation":{"title":"Technical Guidance | Fabric Catalyst","url":"https://microsoft.github.io/fabriccatalyst/DevOps_docs/"}},{"text":"【3:6†source】","start_index":3325,"end_index":3337,"url_citation":{"title":"Microsoft Fabric: CI/CD considerations - Twyzer","url":"https://twyzer.nl/microsoft-fabric-ci-cd-considerations/"}},{"text":"【3:0†source】","start_index":4971,"end_index":4983,"url_citation":{"title":"The Deployment Blueprint: A Practical CI/CD Strategy for Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://formula5.com/the-deployment-blueprint-a-practical-ci-cd-strategy-for-microsoft-fabric/"}},{"text":"【3:4†source】","start_index":4983,"end_index":4995,"url_citation":{"title":"CI/CD Solutions | microsoft/fabric-toolbox | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/microsoft/fabric-toolbox/5.2-cicd-solutions"}},{"text":"【3:2†source】","start_index":5056,"end_index":5068,"url_citation":{"title":"CI/CD workflow options in Fabric - Microsoft Fabric","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/manage-deployment"}},{"text":"【3:5†source】","start_index":5144,"end_index":5156,"url_citation":{"title":"Technical Guidance | Fabric Catalyst","url":"https://microsoft.github.io/fabriccatalyst/DevOps_docs/"}},{"text":"【3:6†source】","start_index":5238,"end_index":5250,"url_citation":{"title":"Microsoft Fabric: CI/CD considerations - 46.     font-weight: 700; font-size: 16px; color: var(--accent);
```
```
47.     white-space: nowrap;
48.   }
49.   .topbar .logo span { color: var(--text-muted); font-weight: 400; }
50.   .topbar .controls { display: flex; gap: 8px; margin-left: auto; }
51.   .topbar button {
52.     background: var(--surface2); color: var(--text); border: 1px solid var(--border);
53.     padding: 6px 14px; border-radius: 6px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 13px;
54.     transition: all .15s;
55.   }
56.   .topbar button:hover { background: var(--border); }
57.   .topbar button.active { background: var(--accent); color: #000; border-color: var(--accent); }
58.   .topbar .timer {
59.     font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Consolas', monospace;
60.     font-size: 18px; color: var(--accent2); min-width: 70px; text-align: center;
61.   }
62. 
63.   /* ─── PROGRESS BAR ─── */
64.   .progress-track {
65.     position: fixed; top: 56px; left: 0; right: 0; z-index: 99;
66.     height: 4px; background: var(--border);
67.   }
68.   .progress-fill {
69.     height: 100%; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent), var(--accent2));
70.     transition: width .3s;
71.   }
72. 
73.   /* ─── SIDEBAR ─── */
74.   .sidebar {
75.     position: fixed; left: 0; top: 60px; bottom: 0; width: 280px;
76.     background: var(--surface); border-right: 1px solid var(--border);
77.     overflow-y: auto; padding: 16px 0; z-index: 50;
78.   }
79.   .sidebar h3 {
80.     padding: 4px 20px; font-size: 11px; text-transform: uppercase;
81.     letter-spacing: 1px; color: var(--text-muted); margin-top: 12px;
82.   }
83.   .sidebar a {
84.     display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;
85.     padding: 6px 20px; color: var(--text-muted); text-decoration: none;
86.     font-size: 13px; transition: all .15s; border-left: 3px solid transparent;
87.   }
88.   .sidebar a:hover { background: var(--surface2); color: var(--text); }
89.   .sidebar a.active {
90.     background: var(--surface2); color: var(--accent);
91.     border-left-color: var(--accent); font-weight: 600;
92.   }
93.   .sidebar a .check { color: var(--accent2); }
94.   .sidebar a .num {
95.     background: var(--border); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px;
96.     font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600;
97.   }
98. 
99.   /* ─── MAIN ─── */
100.   main {
101.     margin-left: 280px; margin-top: 60px; padding: 32px 48px 100px;
102.     max-width: 900px;
103.   }
104. 
105.   /* ─── STEP CARDS ─── */
106.   .part-header {
107.     margin-top: 48px; margin-bottom: 24px;
108.     padding: 20px 24px; border-radius: 12px;
109.     background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--surface2), var(--surface));
110.     border: 1px solid var(--border);
111.   }
112.   .part-header:first-child { margin-top: 0; }
113.   .part-header .part-num {
114.     font-size: 12px; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 2px;
115.     color: var(--accent); font-weight: 700; margin-bottom: 4px;
116.   }
117.   .part-header h2 { font-size: 22px; color: var(--text); margin-bottom: 4px; }
118.   .part-header .duration {
119.     font-size: 13px; color: var(--text-muted);
120.   }
121.   .part-header .goal {
122.     margin-top: 8px; padding: 8px 12px; background: var(--note-bg);
123.     border-left: 3px solid var(--accent); border-radius: 4px;
124.     font-size: 13px; color: var(--text-muted);
125.   }
126. 
127.   .step {
128.     background: var(--surface);
129.     border: 1px solid var(--border);
130.     border-radius: 10px;
131.     margin-bottom: 20px;
132.     overflow: hidden;
133.     transition: border-color .2s;
134.   }
135.   .step.current { border-color: var(--accent); box-shadow: 0 0 0 1px var(--accent); }
136.   .step.done { opacity: 0.6; }
137.   .step.done .step-head .step-status { color: var(--accent2); }
138. 
139.   .step-head {
140.     display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px;
141.     padding: 14px 20px; cursor: pointer;
142.     user-select: none; transition: background .15s;
143.   }
144.   .step-head:hover { background: var(--surface2); }
145.   .step-head .step-num {
146.     background: var(--accent); color: #000; font-weight: 700;
147.     width: 28px; height: 28px; border-radius: 50%;
148.     display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
149.     font-size: 13px; flex-shrink: 0;
150.   }
151.   .step.done .step-head .step-num { background: var(--accent2); }
152.   .step-head .step-title { font-weight: 600; font-size: 15px; flex: 1; }
153.   .step-head .step-screen {
154.     font-size: 12px; color: var(--purple); background: rgba(188,140,255,.1);
155.     padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px;
156.   }
157.   .step-head .step-status { font-size: 18px; }
158. 
159.   .step-body { padding: 0 20px 20px; }
160.   .step-body.collapsed { display: none; }
161. 
162.   /* ─── ACTIONS LIST ─── */
163.   .action {
164.     display: flex; gap: 12px; align-items: flex-start;
165.     padding: 8px 0; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(48,54,61,.5);
166.   }
167.   .action:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
168.   .action-check {
169.     width: 20px; height: 20px; border: 2px solid var(--border);
170.     border-radius: 4px; cursor: pointer; flex-shrink: 0;
171.     margin-top: 2px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
172.     transition: all .15s;
173.   }
174.   .action-check:hover { border-color: var(--accent); }
175.   .action-check.checked {
176.     background: var(--accent2); border-color: var(--accent2);
177.   }
178.   .action-check.checked::after { content: '✓'; color: #000; font-size: 13px; font-weight: 700; }
179.   .action-content { flex: 1; }
180.   .action-content .action-do {
181.     font-size: 14px; color: var(--text);
182.   }
183.   .action-content .action-do strong { color: var(--accent); }
184.   .action-content .action-do code {
185.     background: var(--surface2); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px;
186.     font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Consolas', monospace; font-size: 13px;
187.     color: var(--warning);
188.   }
189. 
190.   /* ─── SPEAKER NOTES ─── */
191.   .notes {
192.     margin-top: 10px; padding: 12px 16px;
193.     background: var(--note-bg); border: 1px solid var(--note-border);
194.     border-radius: 8px; font-size: 13px; color: var(--text-muted);
195.     line-height: 1.7;
196.     transition: all .2s;
197.   }
198.   .notes .notes-label {
199.     font-size: 10px; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 1px;
200.     color: var(--accent); font-weight: 700; margin-bottom: 6px;
201.   }
202.   .notes.hidden { display: none; }
203.   .notes blockquote {
204.     border-left: 3px solid var(--accent);
205.     padding: 8px 12px; margin: 8px 0;
206.     background: rgba(88,166,255,.05); border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
207.     color: var(--text); font-style: italic;
208.   }
209. 
210.   /* ─── TIPS & WARNINGS ─── */
211.   .tip {
212.     margin: 10px 0; padding: 10px 14px;
213.     background: var(--tip-bg); border: 1px solid var(--tip-border);
214.     border-radius: 6px; font-size: 13px;
215.   }
216.   .tip::before { content: '💡 '; }
217.   .warn {
218.     margin: 10px 0; padding: 10px 14px;
219.     background: var(--warn-bg); border: 1px solid var(--warn-border);
220.     border-radius: 6px; font-size: 13px; color: var(--warning);
221.   }
222.   .warn::before { content: '⚠️ '; }
223. 
224.   /* ─── CODE BLOCKS ─── */
225.   pre {
226.     background: #0d1117; border: 1px solid var(--border);
227.     border-radius: 8px; padding: 14px 16px; overflow-x: auto;
228.     font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Consolas', monospace;
229.     font-size: 13px; line-height: 1.5; margin: 8px 0;
230.     color: var(--text);
231.   }
232.   pre .comment { color: var(--text-muted); }
233.   pre .keyword { color: var(--danger); }
234.   pre .string { color: var(--accent2); }
235. 
236.   /* ─── KEY LINKS ─── */
237.   .links-bar {
238.     display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 8px; margin-bottom: 24px;
239.   }
240.   .links-bar a {
241.     background: var(--surface2); color: var(--accent); border: 1px solid var(--border);
242.     padding: 6px 14px; border-radius: 6px; text-decoration: none;
243.     font-size: 13px; transition: all .15s;
244.   }
245.   .links-bar a:hover { background: var(--border); }
246. 
247.   /* ─── KEYBOARD SHORTCUTS PANEL ─── */
248.   .shortcuts-modal {
249.     display: none; position: fixed; inset: 0; z-index: 200;
250.     background: rgba(0,0,0,.7); align-items: center; justify-content: center;
251.   }
252.   .shortcuts-modal.show { display: flex; }
253.   .shortcuts-box {
254.     background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--border);
255.     border-radius: 12px; padding: 24px 32px; max-width: 400px; width: 100%;
256.   }
257.   .shortcuts-box h3 { margin-bottom: 16px; color: var(--accent); }
258.   .shortcuts-box .row {
259.     display: flex; justify-content: space-between; padding: 6px 0;
260.     border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border); font-size: 14px;
```

</details>

```
282. 
283. <!-- ═══ TOP BAR ═══ -->
284. <div class="topbar">
285.   <div class="logo">🚀 Fabric CI/CD Demo <span>— Digital Realty</span></div>
286.   <div class="timer" id="timer">00:00</div>
287.   <div class="controls">
288.     <button onclick="toggleTimer()" id="timerBtn">▶ Start Timer</button>
289.     <button onclick="toggleNotes()" id="notesBtn" class="active">👁 Notes ON</button>
290.     <button onclick="toggleShortcuts()">⌨ Keys</button>
291.     <button onclick="resetAll()">↺ Reset</button>
292.   </div>
293. </div>
294. 
295. <!-- ═══ PROGRESS BAR ═══ -->
296. <div class="progress-track"><div class="progress-fill" id="progressFill" style="width:0%"></div></div>
297. 
298. <!-- ═══ KEYBOARD SHORTCUTS ═══ -->
299. <div class="shortcuts-modal" id="shortcutsModal">
300.   <div class="shortcuts-box">
301.     <h3>Keyboard Shortcuts</h3>
302.     <div class="row"><span>Next step</span><kbd>→</kbd> or <kbd>N</kbd></div>
303.     <div class="row"><span>Previous step</span><kbd>←</kbd> or <kbd>P</kbd></div>
304.     <div class="row"><span>Toggle notes</span><kbd>H</kbd></div>
305.     <div class="row"><span>Start/pause timer</span><kbd>T</kbd></div>
306.     <div class="row"><span>Mark step done</span><kbd>D</kbd></div>
307.     <div class="row"><span>Close this panel</span><kbd>Esc</kbd></div>
308.     <div style="margin-top:16px;text-align:center;">
309.       <button onclick="toggleShortcuts()" style="background:var(--accent);color:#000;border:none;padding:8px 20px;border-radius:6px;cursor:pointer;">Got it</button>
310.     </div>
311.   </div>
312. </div>
313. 
314. <!-- ═══ SIDEBAR ═══ -->
315. <nav class="sidebar" id="sidebar"></nav>
316. 
317. <!-- ═══ MAIN CONTENT ═══ -->

320. <script>
321. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
322. // DATA: All demo steps
323. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
324. const DEMO = [
325.   // ─── PART 1: SLIDES ───
326.   {
327.     part: 1, partTitle: "Slides — Context & Problem", duration: "10 min",
328.     goal: "Set the context. Explain the problem before showing the solution. Keep the audience engaged with visuals.",
329.     steps: [
330.       {
331.         id: "1.1", title: "Slide 1 — SDLC Overview",
332.         screen: "PowerPoint",
333.         actions: [
334.           "Open <strong>Fabric_CICD_Demo.pptx</strong> from the <code>presentation</code> folder",
335.           "Press <strong>F5</strong> to start the slideshow (full screen)",
336.           "Show <strong>Slide 1</strong> — the architecture diagram with Dev → UAT → Prod workspaces"
337.         ],
338.         notes: `<blockquote>"Microsoft Fabric provides a unified analytics platform. For SDLC, we use:<br>
339.           • <strong>Workspaces</strong> as our environments (Dev, UAT, Prod)<br>
340.           • <strong>Git integration</strong> to version-control our artifacts<br>
341.           • <strong>Deployment Pipelines</strong> to promote changes across environments<br>
342.           Let me show you how these pieces fit together."</blockquote>`,
343.         tips: ["Pause after each bullet point. Let them absorb.", "Make eye contact while showing the slide — don't read verbatim."]
344.       },
345.       {
346.         id: "1.2", title: "Slide 2 — Your Live Environments",
347.         screen: "PowerPoint",
348.         actions: [
349.           "Press <strong>→</strong> (right arrow) to advance to Slide 2",
350.           "Point to the <strong>3 workspace screenshots</strong> — these are real screenshots from their own tenant",
351.           "Highlight that each workspace has a <strong>DataCenterLakehouse</strong>"
352.         ],
353.         notes: `<blockquote>"These are the actual environments I set up for this demo. Each workspace mirrors your Dev, UAT, and Prod. They all have the same DataCenterLakehouse — but right now, they're empty shells."</blockquote>`,
354.         tips: ["The screenshots are real — mention this! It builds credibility."]
355.       },
356.       {
357.         id: "1.3", title: "Slide 3 — What Git Tracks",
358.         screen: "PowerPoint",
359.         actions: [
360.           "Press <strong>→</strong> to advance to Slide 3",
361.           "Point to the <strong>✅ Tracked</strong> column — notebooks, pipelines, semantic models, reports",
362.           "Point to the <strong>❌ Not Tracked</strong> column — Lakehouse tables, views, schemas, stored procedures"
363.         ],
364.         notes: `<blockquote>"Git integration works great for most artifacts — notebooks, data pipelines, semantic models, reports. But there's an important gap: Lakehouse table definitions, schemas, and views are NOT tracked. Only the Lakehouse container metadata is serialized."</blockquote>`,
365.         tips: ["This is the 'aha' moment. Pause after saying 'NOT tracked' — let it sink in."]
366.       },
367.       {
368.         id: "1.4", title: "Slide 4 — The Schema Gap",
369.         screen: "PowerPoint",
370.         actions: [
371.           "Press <strong>→</strong> to advance to Slide 4",
372.           "Walk through the visual: Dev Lakehouse has tables → Git captures NO tables → UAT gets empty Lakehouse",
373.           "Emphasize: <strong>This is the exact problem you're facing</strong>"
374.         ],
375.         notes: `<blockquote>"Here's the problem you described to us. When someone creates a table in the Dev Lakehouse — through the UI or SQL endpoint — that change stays in Dev only. Git doesn't capture it. Deployment Pipelines don't propagate it. The result is schema drift: your environments get out of sync."</blockquote>`,
376.         tips: ["Reference their original email here — show you listened.", "Use the word 'drift' — it resonates with ops teams."]
377.       },
378.       {
379.         id: "1.5", title: "Slide 5 — Schema-as-Code Solution",
380.         screen: "PowerPoint",
381.         actions: [
382.           "Press <strong>→</strong> to advance to Slide 5",
383.           "Show the solution architecture: Migration scripts in Git → CI/CD pipeline → Fabric notebook → Lakehouse"
384.         ],
385.         notes: `<blockquote>"The recommended approach is Schema-as-Code. We write migration scripts — SQL or PySpark — that define our table structures. These scripts live in Git. Our CI/CD pipeline runs these scripts against each environment. Combined with Deployment Pipelines for other artifacts, we get full coverage."</blockquote>`,
386.         tips: ["Don't rush this slide — it's the solution reveal.", "Say: 'This is what the product team recommends.'"]
387.       },
388.       {
389.         id: "1.6", title: "Slide 6 — Deployment Pipeline (Live)",
390.         screen: "PowerPoint",
391.         actions: [
392.           "Press <strong>→</strong> to advance to Slide 6",
393.           "Show the <strong>real screenshot</strong> of the DigitalRealty-Pipeline with all 3 stages assigned"
394.         ],
395.         notes: `<blockquote>"This is the actual Deployment Pipeline I created in your tenant. Dev → Test → Production, all linked to the correct workspaces. This handles the standard artifact promotion. For schemas, we add the migration layer on top."</blockquote>`,
396.         tips: []
397.       },
398.       {
399.         id: "1.7", title: "Slide 7 — Demo Roadmap",
400.         screen: "PowerPoint",
401.         actions: [
402.           "Press <strong>→</strong> to advance to Slide 7",
403.           "Read through the roadmap so they know what's coming",
404.           "Press <strong>Esc</strong> to exit slideshow when done"
405.         ],
406.         notes: `<blockquote>"Let me show you this working end-to-end. We'll walk through: 1) The environment setup, 2) Standard Git CI/CD, 3) The Lakehouse gap — the problem, 4) Schema-as-Code — the solution, 5) REST API automation. Let's switch to the live environment."</blockquote>`,
407.         tips: ["Good transition moment. Take a sip of water.", "Switch your browser to the Fabric tab now."]
408.       }
409.     ]
410.   },
411. 
412.   // ─── PART 2: ENVIRONMENT TOUR ───
413.   {
414.     part: 2, partTitle: "Live — Environment Tour", duration: "8 min",
415.     goal: "Walk them through the actual Fabric environments you set up. Build confidence that this is real, not slides.",
416.     steps: [
417.       {
418.         id: "2.1", title: "Show Fabric Workspaces",
419.         screen: "Fabric Portal",
420.         actions: [
421.           "Open your browser with <strong>https://app.fabric.microsoft.com</strong>",
422.           "Click <strong>Workspaces</strong> in the left sidebar",
423.           "Click <strong>DigitalRealty-Dev</strong> — show the DataCenterLakehouse inside",
424.           "Click <strong>← Back</strong> → click <strong>DigitalRealty-UAT</strong> — show it's the same structure",
425.           "Click <strong>← Back</strong> → click <strong>DigitalRealty-Prod</strong> — show it's the same"
426.         ],
427.         notes: `<blockquote>"We have 3 workspaces mirroring your environments — Dev, UAT, and Prod. Each has a DataCenterLakehouse. Right now they're empty — no tables yet. That's intentional. We'll create schemas through code."</blockquote>`,
428.         tips: ["Zoom browser to 125% so the audience can read text easily.", "Collapse side panes if they clutter the view."]
429.       },
430.       {
431.         id: "2.2", title: "Show the Deployment Pipeline",
432.         screen: "Fabric Portal",
433.         actions: [
434.           "Click the <strong>⚙ gear icon</strong> in the bottom-left corner of Fabric",
435.           "Click <strong>Deployment pipelines</strong> in the menu",
436.           "Click on <strong>DigitalRealty-Pipeline</strong>",
437.           "Point out the 3 stages: <strong>Development → Test → Production</strong>",
438.           "Show each stage is linked to the correct workspace"
439.         ],
440.         notes: `<blockquote>"This is Fabric's built-in Deployment Pipeline. It lets us promote artifacts from Dev to Test to Production with one click. We'll use this for notebooks, pipelines, and other standard artifacts. For schema changes, we'll add an extra layer."</blockquote>`,
441.         tips: ["If the audience asks 'why not just use this for everything?' — that's your cue to tease Part 4."]
442.       },
443.       {
444.         id: "2.3", title: "Show the Azure DevOps Repo",
445.         screen: "Azure DevOps",
446.         actions: [
447.           "Open a <strong>new browser tab</strong> → go to your Azure DevOps repo",
448.           "Show the folder structure: <code>lakehouse-migrations/</code>, <code>pipelines/</code>, <code>scripts/</code>, <code>notebooks/</code>",
449.           "Click into <code>lakehouse-migrations/migrations/</code> — show the SQL and Python files"
450.         ],
451.         notes: `<blockquote>"Here's our Azure DevOps repo. Notice the lakehouse-migrations folder — this is where we store our schema-as-code. Each version is a separate file — v001 for baseline, v002 for evolution."</blockquote>`,
452.         tips: ["If you haven't pushed the repo yet, show the local folder structure on your machine instead."]
453.       },
454.       {
455.         id: "2.4", title: "Show Git Integration in Dev Workspace",
```

```
697.           "→ <strong>trigger_notebook()</strong> — Triggers a notebook run via Job Scheduler API",

699.         ],

708.           "Run <strong>Cell 1 — Authenticate</strong>:",

710.           "Run <strong>Cell 2 — List Workspaces</strong>:",
711.           "<pre>r = requests.get(\"https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces\", headers=headers)\nfor ws in r.json()[\"value\"]:\n    print(f\"  {ws['displayName']} ({ws['id']})\")</pre>",
712.           "Run <strong>Cell 3 — List Items in UAT</strong>:",

714.         ],

732.   // ─── WRAP-UP ───
733.   {
734.     part: 7, partTitle: "Wrap-Up & Q&A", duration: "5 min",
735.     goal: "Summarize the key takeaways. Open the floor for questions. Leave them confident in the approach.",
736.     steps: [
737.       {
738.         id: "7.1", title: "Summary Slide",
739.         screen: "PowerPoint",
740.         actions: [
741.           "Switch back to <strong>PowerPoint</strong> → go to <strong>Slide 5</strong> (Solution Architecture)",
742.           "Or just summarize verbally with the Fabric portal still visible"
743.         ],
744.         notes: `<blockquote>"Let me summarize what we've shown today:<br><br>
745.           1. <strong>Standard artifacts</strong> (notebooks, pipelines, reports) → Git + Deployment Pipelines work perfectly.<br>
746.           2. <strong>Lakehouse schemas</strong> → Not tracked by Git today. This is a known gap.<br>
747.           3. <strong>Schema-as-Code</strong> → Versioned migration scripts, run by a migration notebook, triggered by CI/CD.<br>
748.           4. <strong>Both Azure DevOps and GitHub</strong> work for this.<br>
749.           5. <strong>Fabric REST APIs</strong> power all the automation.<br><br>
750.           One more important note: <strong>Fabric Warehouse</strong> DOES support object-level Git integration. If you need deep schema versioning natively, consider using Warehouse for structured tables and Lakehouse for unstructured/semi-structured data."</blockquote>`,
751.         tips: ["The Warehouse mention is a strong closing point — it shows you know the full platform.", "End on a positive: 'The gap has a solution, and we just showed it working.'"]
752.       },
753.       {
754.         id: "7.2", title: "Q&A",
755.         screen: "Any — be ready to switch",
756.         actions: [
757.           "Say: <strong>\"Happy to go deeper on governance, APIs, or the migration runner.\"</strong>",
758.           "Keep Fabric portal, Azure DevOps, GitHub, and docs tabs ready",
759.           "Share the repo link and documentation links with the audience"
760.         ],
761.         notes: `<blockquote>"Questions? I'm happy to dive deeper into any aspect — the migration approach, the CI/CD pipeline setup, the REST APIs, or how this fits into your existing workflows."</blockquote>
762.           <br><strong>Common questions to prepare for:</strong><br>
763.           • "What about rollbacks?" → Migration scripts can include DOWN migrations; version the rollback too.<br>
764.           • "What if someone creates tables via UI?" → The migration runner is the source of truth; UI changes should be captured as new migration scripts.<br>
765.           • "Can we use Warehouse instead?" → Yes! Warehouse has full Git integration for schemas. Consider it for structured data.<br>
766.           • "How do we handle data along with schema?" → Seed scripts can be part of migrations; or use separate data pipelines.<br>
767.           • "What about permissions/RBAC?" → Service Principal needs Contributor on each workspace.`,
768.         tips: ["Don't end early — Q&A is where trust is built.", "If no questions, ask them: 'What's your current pain point?'"]
769.       }
770.     ]
771.   }
772. ];
773. 
774. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
775. // STATE
776. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
777. let notesVisible = true;
778. let timerRunning = false;
779. let timerSeconds = 0;
780. let timerInterval = null;
781. let currentStepIdx = 0;
782. let completedSteps = new Set();
783. 
784. // Flatten steps for navigation
785. const allSteps = [];
786. DEMO.forEach(part => part.steps.forEach(step => allSteps.push({ ...step, part: part.part, partTitle: part.partTitle })));
787. const totalSteps = allSteps.length;
788. 
789. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
790. // RENDER
791. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
792. function render() {
793.   renderSidebar();
794.   renderMain();
795.   updateProgress();
796. }
797. 
798. function renderSidebar() {
799.   const sb = document.getElementById('sidebar');
800.   let html = '';
801.   DEMO.forEach(part => {
802.     html += `<h3>Part ${part.part}: ${part.partTitle}</h3>`;
803.     part.steps.forEach(step => {
804.       const idx = allSteps.findIndex(s => s.id === step.id);
805.       const active = idx === currentStepIdx ? 'active' : '';
806.       const check = completedSteps.has(step.id) ? '<span class="check">✓</span>' : '';
807.       html += `<a href="#step-${step.id}" class="${active}" onclick="goToStep(${idx}); return false;">
808.         <span class="num">${step.id}</span> ${step.title} ${check}
809.       </a>`;
810.     });
811.   });
812.   sb.innerHTML = html;
813. }
814. 
815. function renderMain() {
816.   const m = document.getElementById('main');
817.   let html = '';
818. 
819.   // Quick links
820.   html += `<div class="links-bar">
821.     <a href="https://app.fabric.microsoft.com" target="_blank">🌐 Fabric Portal</a>
822.     <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/fabric/" target="_blank">📘 Fabric REST API</a>
823.     <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/git-integration/git-automation" target="_blank">📘 Git Automation</a>
824.     <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/deployment-pipelines/pipeline-automation-fabric" target="_blank">📘 Pipeline API</a>
825.   </div>`;
826. 
827.   let lastPart = 0;
828.   allSteps.forEach((step, idx) => {
829.     // Part header
830.     if (step.part !== lastPart) {
831.       lastPart = step.part;
832.       const part = DEMO.find(p => p.part === step.part);
833.       html += `<div class="part-header" id="part-${step.part}">
834.         <div class="part-num">Part ${part.part} of ${DEMO.length}</div>
835.         <h2>${part.partTitle}</h2>
836.         <div class="duration">⏱ ${part.duration}</div>
837.         <div class="goal">🎯 ${part.goal}</div>
838.       </div>`;
839.     }
840. 
841.     const isCurrent = idx === currentStepIdx;
842.     const isDone = completedSteps.has(step.id);
843.     const cls = isCurrent ? 'current' : isDone ? 'done' : '';
844.     const bodyClass = (isCurrent || isDone) ? '' : 'collapsed';
845.     const statusIcon = isDone ? '✅' : isCurrent ? '👉' : '○';
846. 
847.     html += `<div class="step ${cls}" id="step-${step.id}">
848.       <div class="step-head" onclick="goToStep(${idx})">
849.         <div class="step-num">${step.id}</div>
850.         <div class="step-title">${step.title}</div>
851.         <div class="step-screen">${step.screen}</div>
852.         <div class="step-status">${statusIcon}</div>
853.       </div>
854.       <div class="step-body ${bodyClass}">`;
855. 
856.     // Actions
857.     step.actions.forEach((action, ai) => {
858.       const checked = isDone ? 'checked' : '';
859.       html += `<div class="action">
860.         <div class="action-check ${checked}" onclick="toggleAction(event, '${step.id}', ${ai})"></div>
861.         <div class="action-content"><div class="action-do">${action}</div></div>
862.       </div>`;
863.     });
864. 
865.     // Tips
866.     if (step.tips && step.tips.length > 0) {
867.       step.tips.forEach(tip => {
868.         html += `<div class="tip">${tip}</div>`;
869.       });
870.     }
871. 
872.     // Speaker notes
873.     if (step.notes) {
874.       html += `<div class="notes ${notesVisible ? '' : 'hidden'}">
875.         <div class="notes-label">🎤 What to say</div>
876.         ${step.notes}
877.       </div>`;
878.     }
879. 
880.     html += `</div></div>`;
881.   });
882. 
883.   m.innerHTML = html;
884. }
885. 
886. function updateProgress() {
887.   const pct = totalSteps > 0 ? (completedSteps.size / totalSteps * 100) : 0;
888.   document.getElementById('progressFill').style.width = pct + '%';
889. }
890. 
891. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
892. // NAVIGATION
893. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
894. function goToStep(idx) {
895.   if (idx < 0 || idx >= totalSteps) return;
896.   currentStepIdx = idx;
897.   render();
898.   const el = document.getElementById('step-' + allSteps[idx].id);
899.   if (el) el.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });
900. }
901. 
902. function nextStep() {
903.   // Mark current as done
904.   completedSteps.add(allSteps[currentStepIdx].id);
905.   if (currentStepIdx < totalSteps - 1) {
906.     goToStep(currentStepIdx + 1);
907.   } else {
908.     render();
909.   }
910. }
911. 
912. function prevStep() {
913.   if (currentStepIdx > 0) goToStep(currentStepIdx - 1);
914. }
915. 
916. function markDone() {
917.   const step = allSteps[currentStepIdx];
918.   if (completedSteps.has(step.id)) {
919.     completedSteps.delete(step.id);
920.   } else {
921.     completedSteps.add(step.id);
922.   }
923.   render();
924. }
925. 
926. function toggleAction(e, stepId, actionIdx) {
927.   e.stopPropagation();
928.   const el = e.target;
929.   el.classList.toggle('checked');
930. }
931. 
932. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
933. // TIMER
934. // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
935. function toggleTimer() {
936.   if (timerRunning) {
937.     clearInterval(timerInterval);
938.     timerRunning = false;
939.     document.getElementById('timerBtn').textContent = '▶ Resume';
940.   } else {
941.     timerInterval = setInterval(() => {
942.       timerSeconds++;
```
