  "command": "@'\nfrom pathlib import Path\nimport json\n\npath = Path(r\"C:\\Users\\sautalwar\\Downloads\\repos\\Fabric_GitHub\\demo-guide.html\")\nold_text = path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\nstart = old_text.index(\"const DEMO = [\")\nstart_arr = old_text.index(\"[\", start)\ndepth = 0\nin_string = None\nescape = False\nend_arr = None\nfor i in range(start_arr, len(old_text)):\n    ch = old_text[i]\n    if in_string:\n        if escape:\n            escape = False\n        elif ch == \"\\\\\":\n            escape = True\n        elif ch == in_string:\n            in_string = None\n    else:\n        if ch in ('\\\"', \"'\", '`'):\n            in_string = ch\n        elif ch == '[':\n            depth += 1\n        elif ch == ']':\n            depth -= 1\n            if depth == 0:\n                end_arr = i + 1\n                break\nif end_arr is None:\n    raise RuntimeError(\"Could not extract DEMO array\")\ndemo_source = old_text[start_arr:end_arr]\n\nknowledge_base = [\n    {\n        \"id\": \"fabric\",\n        \"title\": \"What is Microsoft Fabric?\",\n        \"summary\": \"Fabric unifies analytics, engineering, BI, and governance into one SaaS platform.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>Microsoft Fabric is a unified analytics platform that brings together data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, data warehousing, and business intelligence into a single SaaS product.</p><p>It's built on OneLake (a single data lake for the entire organization) and uses a shared capacity model. Think of it as Azure Synapse + Power BI + Data Factory + Data Activator all unified under one roof with a single security model, billing model, and governance layer.</p>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"lakehouse\",\n        \"title\": \"What is a Lakehouse?\",\n        \"summary\": \"A Lakehouse combines open data-lake storage with warehouse-style analytics access.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>A Lakehouse combines the best of data lakes and data warehouses. It stores data as Delta Lake files in OneLake (like a data lake) but provides a SQL analytics endpoint for querying (like a warehouse).</p><ul><li><strong>Storage:</strong> Delta Parquet files in OneLake</li><li><strong>Compute:</strong> Apache Spark for data engineering, SQL endpoint for analytics</li><li><strong>Schema:</strong> Schema-on-read (flexible) + schema enforcement via Delta Lake</li><li><strong>Format:</strong> Open Delta Lake format (no vendor lock-in)</li><li><strong>Access:</strong> Notebooks (PySpark/Scala/SQL), SQL endpoint (T-SQL read-only), Power BI (Direct Lake mode)</li><li><strong>Use cases:</strong> Data engineering, ETL/ELT pipelines, data science, machine learning, medallion architecture (bronze/silver/gold)</li></ul>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"delta-lake\",\n        \"title\": \"What is Delta Lake?\",\n        \"summary\": \"Delta Lake is the transaction layer that gives Fabric Lakehouses reliability and history.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that brings ACID transactions to data lakes. Every table in a Fabric Lakehouse is a Delta table.</p><ul><li><strong>ACID Transactions:</strong> Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — no partial writes</li><li><strong>Schema Enforcement:</strong> Rejects writes that don't match the schema</li><li><strong>Schema Evolution:</strong> Can add new columns with <code>mergeSchema</code> option</li><li><strong>Time Travel:</strong> Query any previous version of the data (up to retention period)</li><li><strong>Audit History:</strong> Full transaction log (<code>_delta_log</code>) records every change</li><li><strong>Optimized Writes:</strong> Auto-compaction, Z-ordering, V-ordering for performance</li></ul>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"lakehouse-vs-warehouse\",\n        \"title\": \"Lakehouse vs Warehouse in Fabric\",\n        \"summary\": \"Use the right engine for the right layer — and mix both when needed.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<table><thead><tr><th>Aspect</th><th>Lakehouse</th><th>Warehouse</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Data format</td><td>Delta Lake (open Parquet)</td><td>Proprietary database pages</td></tr><tr><td>Write access</td><td>Spark notebooks, Dataflows</td><td>Full T-SQL DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)</td></tr><tr><td>SQL access</td><td>Read-only SQL endpoint</td><td>Full read-write T-SQL</td></tr><tr><td>Git integration (schemas)</td><td>Container metadata only ❌</td><td>Full object-level (tables, views, procs) ✅</td></tr><tr><td>Best for</td><td>Data engineering, ML, raw data</td><td>BI reporting, curated data, SQL workloads</td></tr><tr><td>Data types</td><td>Structured + semi-structured + unstructured</td><td>Structured only</td></tr><tr><td>Power BI mode</td><td>Direct Lake (fastest)</td><td>DirectQuery or Import</td></tr><tr><td>Multi-table transactions</td><td>No</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td>Cross-database queries</td><td>No</td><td>Yes</td></tr></tbody></table><div class=\\\"knowledge-callout\\\"><strong>Key insight for this demo:</strong> Warehouse has full Git integration for schemas. If the customer needs native schema versioning, Warehouse is the better fit for that specific layer. Many enterprises use BOTH: Lakehouse for data engineering (bronze/silver) and Warehouse for curated serving (gold layer).</div>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"workspace\",\n        \"title\": \"What is a Fabric Workspace?\",\n        \"summary\": \"A workspace is the environment boundary for security, capacity, Git, and item organization.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>A workspace is a logical container in Fabric that holds related items (Lakehouses, Warehouses, notebooks, pipelines, reports). Think of it as a <em>project folder</em> with:</p><ul><li><strong>Access control:</strong> Role-based (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer)</li><li><strong>Capacity binding:</strong> Each workspace is assigned to a Fabric capacity</li><li><strong>Git integration:</strong> Each workspace can connect to a Git branch</li><li><strong>Environment mapping:</strong> Dev workspace → dev branch, UAT workspace → uat branch, etc.</li></ul>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"deployment-pipelines\",\n        \"title\": \"What are Deployment Pipelines?\",\n        \"summary\": \"Deployment Pipelines promote supported Fabric artifacts between isolated workspaces.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>Fabric Deployment Pipelines are a built-in feature for promoting artifacts between workspaces (typically Dev → Test → Prod).</p><ul><li>Compare artifacts between stages and show differences</li><li>Deploy changed artifacts with one click or via API</li><li>Support deployment rules for environment-specific config (connection strings, parameters)</li><li>Do <strong>NOT</strong> deploy data or table schemas — only artifact definitions</li><li>Require Premium/Fabric capacity (F64+ or Trial)</li></ul>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"git-integration\",\n        \"title\": \"What is Git Integration in Fabric?\",\n        \"summary\": \"Git integration serializes supported Fabric items into source control for branching and review.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>Fabric workspaces can connect to Azure DevOps or GitHub repositories. When connected:</p><ul><li>Artifacts are serialized to JSON/definition files in the repo</li><li>Changes can be committed from workspace → Git</li><li>Updates can be pulled from Git → workspace</li><li>Enables branch-based development, code review via PRs, and version history</li><li><strong>Limitation:</strong> Only artifact definitions are tracked, <strong>NOT</strong> data or Lakehouse table schemas</li></ul>\"\n    },\n    {\n        \"id\": \"onelake\",\n        \"title\": \"What is OneLake?\",\n        \"summary\": \"OneLake is Fabric's shared storage foundation — one lake, many engines.\",\n        \"contentHtml\": \"<p>OneLake is Fabric's unified data lake — a single storage layer for the entire organization. Think <em>OneDrive for data.</em> Every Fabric item stores its data in OneLake.</p><ul><li>Single namespace across the org</li><li>Data stored once, accessed by all engines (Spark, SQL, Power BI)</li><li>Supports shortcuts to external data (ADLS, S3, GCS) without copying</li><li>Delta Lake format by default</li><li>Governed by workspace-level and item-level security</li></ul>\"\n    }\n]\n\nqa_by_part = {\n    1: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q1\",\n            \"question\": \"You said Lakehouse table schemas are not tracked by Git. What EXACTLY does Git track for a Lakehouse?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Git tracks only the Lakehouse container metadata. Specifically:</p><ul><li>Display name and logical ID (GUID) of the Lakehouse</li><li>OneLake shortcut definitions (if any)</li><li>Data Access Roles (preview feature) — the security configuration</li><li>Default <code>dbo</code> schema container metadata</li></ul><p>That's it. The actual <code>CREATE TABLE</code> statements, column definitions, data types, views, stored procedures, functions — <strong>NONE</strong> of that is serialized. When you look in Git, you'll see a folder with a few JSON files — <code>.platform</code>, <code>lakehouse.metadata.json</code>, maybe <code>data-access-roles.json</code>. But no DDL.</p>\",\n            \"impactHtml\": \"<p>This means if someone creates 50 tables in Dev via the SQL endpoint, commits to Git, and deploys to UAT — UAT gets an empty Lakehouse with the same name but zero tables.</p>\",\n            \"demoHtml\": \"<p><strong>Want me to show you?</strong> Let me create a table right now and check Git. That is exactly what Part 4 demonstrates.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q2\",\n            \"question\": \"Why doesn't Microsoft just track table schemas in Git like they do for Warehouse?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>It's an architectural difference. A Warehouse stores objects as SQL metadata (like SQL Server) — tables, views, and procs are first-class database objects that can be extracted as DDL scripts. A Lakehouse stores data as Delta Lake files in OneLake — the <em>schema</em> is embedded in the Delta transaction log (<code>_delta_log</code> folder) inside each table's folder, not in a central metadata catalog.</p><p>Extracting and serializing those Delta logs into a Git-friendly format is technically more complex. Microsoft has acknowledged this gap and it's on their roadmap. In the meantime, Schema-as-Code is the recommended pattern.</p>\",\n            \"followUpHtml\": \"<p><strong>Should we switch to Warehouse then?</strong> Not necessarily. Lakehouse gives you Direct Lake mode for Power BI (fastest query performance), flexible schema evolution, Spark processing, and support for semi-structured data. Many enterprises use <strong>BOTH</strong>: Lakehouse for data engineering (bronze/silver layers) and Warehouse for curated serving (gold layer) where Git integration gives you full schema tracking.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q3\",\n            \"question\": \"What is the medallion architecture and how does this fit in?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>The medallion architecture organizes data into three layers:</p><ul><li><strong>Bronze (Raw):</strong> Raw data as ingested — minimal transformation, full fidelity</li><li><strong>Silver (Cleansed):</strong> Cleaned, validated, conformed data — deduped, typed, joined</li><li><strong>Gold (Curated):</strong> Business-ready aggregations, KPIs, reporting tables</li></ul><p>In Fabric, you might use a Lakehouse for Bronze and Silver (where flexible schema and Spark processing shine) and either a Lakehouse or Warehouse for Gold (where SQL access and BI reporting matter most). The Schema-as-Code approach applies at every layer — you want every table creation versioned regardless of the layer.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q4\",\n            \"question\": \"What about data governance? If schemas aren't tracked, how do we ensure compliance?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>This is a critical concern. Without schema tracking:</p><ul><li>No audit trail for who created or modified a table and when (unless you check Delta history manually)</li><li>No code review process for schema changes (someone can <code>ALTER TABLE</code> in the UI without approval)</li><li>No way to enforce naming conventions or standards through PR reviews</li><li>No rollback capability through Git — you'd have to manually write a reversal script</li></ul><p>Schema-as-Code solves all of these: every change goes through a Git PR, gets reviewed, has an audit trail, and can be rolled back by reverting the commit.</p>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    2: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q5\",\n            \"question\": \"Why 3 separate workspaces instead of using branches in one workspace?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Fabric workspaces are the isolation boundary. Each workspace:</p><ul><li>Has its own compute resources (Spark sessions, SQL endpoint)</li><li>Has independent access controls (different people can access Dev vs Prod)</li><li>Can connect to different data sources (Dev database vs Prod database)</li><li>Has its own Lakehouse with its own OneLake storage</li></ul><p>Using branches in one workspace doesn't give you this isolation. The pattern is: one workspace per environment, each connected to a different Git branch (main → Dev, release → UAT, production → Prod). Deployment Pipelines handle the promotion between workspaces.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q6\",\n            \"question\": \"What's the difference between Deployment Pipelines and Git-based CI/CD? Do we need both?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>They serve different purposes and complement each other:</p><ul><li><strong>Deployment Pipelines:</strong> Promote Fabric-native artifacts (notebooks, pipelines, reports, semantic models) between workspaces. Visual, one-click. Handles environment-specific configuration rules. But does <strong>NOT</strong> handle table schemas or data.</li><li><strong>Git-based CI/CD:</strong> Handles everything that Deployment Pipelines can't — schema migrations, data seeding, custom validations, approval gates, notifications. Code-centric, scriptable, auditable.</li></ul><p>Best practice: use <strong>BOTH</strong>. Deployment Pipelines for standard artifacts. Git-based CI/CD (with Schema-as-Code) for Lakehouse schemas. They're not competing — they're complementary.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q7\",\n            \"question\": \"How do you handle data isolation between environments? Dev shouldn't have production data.\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Each workspace has its own Lakehouse with its own OneLake storage. Data is physically separate. For Dev, you'd typically:</p><ol><li>Use synthetic or anonymized data (generate via notebooks or import sample CSVs)</li><li>Use OneLake shortcuts to reference a subset of production data (read-only, no copy)</li><li>Use Data Pipelines to copy and mask production data into Dev</li></ol><p>The Schema-as-Code approach handles the structure; data population is a separate concern handled by data pipelines or seeding scripts.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q8\",\n            \"question\": \"Can we use Fabric capacities to isolate environments? What about cost?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Yes. Each workspace is bound to a capacity. Options:</p><ul><li><strong>Shared capacity:</strong> All workspaces on one Fabric capacity (cheapest, but Dev load can impact Prod performance)</li><li><strong>Separate capacities:</strong> Dev on F2, UAT on F4, Prod on F64 (best isolation, higher cost)</li><li><strong>Trial capacity:</strong> What we're using today — free but limited and temporary</li></ul><p>For production workloads, separate capacities (or at least Dev/Test on one, Prod on another) is recommended.</p>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    3: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q9\",\n            \"question\": \"What happens if two developers commit to Git at the same time? How are conflicts handled?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Fabric uses a workspace-level lock. Only one user can commit at a time from a workspace. If there's a conflict (someone else committed first), you'll get a merge conflict notification and need to resolve it — similar to any Git workflow.</p><p>Best practices:</p><ul><li>Use feature branches: each developer works in their own branch or workspace</li><li>Merge to main via Pull Requests with reviews</li><li>Sync workspace from Git regularly to stay current</li></ul><p>Currently, Fabric doesn't have great merge conflict resolution UX — so the PR-based workflow is strongly recommended.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q10\",\n            \"question\": \"What specific artifacts does Git integration serialize? Give me the full list.\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>As of the current Fabric release, Git serialization is supported (GA) for:</p><p>✅ Notebooks, Data Pipelines, Spark Job Definitions, Spark Environment<br>✅ Semantic Models (Power BI datasets), Reports, Paginated Reports<br>✅ Warehouses (full object-level: tables, views, procs, functions)<br>✅ Lakehouses (container metadata only — NOT tables or views)<br>✅ Dataflows Gen2, ML Models, ML Experiments</p><p>❌ Lakehouse table schemas, views, stored procedures<br>❌ Real-time dashboards, Eventstreams<br>❌ KQL databases, KQL querysets<br>❌ Data Activator reflexes</p><p>The key takeaway: <strong>Warehouse = full schema tracking. Lakehouse = container only.</strong></p>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    4: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q11\",\n            \"question\": \"This is a serious gap. Is Microsoft going to fix this? What's on the roadmap?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Microsoft has acknowledged this limitation. The Fabric product team is actively working on improved Git integration for Lakehouse. However, there's no confirmed timeline for full Lakehouse schema serialization.</p><p>In the meantime, Schema-as-Code is the officially recommended workaround. It's worth noting that this pattern (database migrations managed as code) is industry-standard — tools like Flyway, Liquibase, Django migrations, and Entity Framework migrations have been doing this for years. So it's not a hack — it's a proven pattern applied to Fabric.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q12\",\n            \"question\": \"What if someone creates tables through the UI and we can't control that? How do we detect schema drift?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Great question. You need a drift detection mechanism:</p><ol><li><strong>Automated detection notebook:</strong> Write a notebook that runs nightly, queries <code>INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES</code> and <code>INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS</code> from each Lakehouse SQL endpoint, and compares against the migration scripts in Git. Any table that exists in the Lakehouse but <strong>NOT</strong> in the migration scripts = unauthorized drift.</li><li><strong>Alerting:</strong> The notebook sends an alert (email, Teams message, or Azure Monitor alert) when drift is detected.</li><li><strong>Policy:</strong> Establish a policy that <strong>ALL</strong> schema changes must go through migration scripts. UI-created tables found by the drift detector must be formalized into a new migration script before the next release.</li><li><strong>Access control:</strong> Restrict SQL endpoint write access in UAT and Prod workspaces so only the CI/CD service principal can make schema changes.</li></ol>\",\n            \"demoHtml\": \"<p><strong>DEMO-WORTHY:</strong> Should I show you what a drift detection query looks like? A quick query against <code>INFORMATION_SCHEMA</code> is a great live follow-up.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q13\",\n            \"question\": \"What about views, stored procedures, and functions in the Lakehouse? Are those tracked?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>No. Spark views created via <code>CREATE VIEW</code> in the SQL endpoint are <strong>NOT</strong> tracked by Git. They exist only in the Lakehouse's SQL endpoint metadata. Stored procedures and functions are also not tracked.</p><p>For views, include them in your migration scripts alongside tables. For complex logic, consider using notebooks (which <strong>ARE</strong> tracked by Git) instead of stored procedures.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q14\",\n            \"question\": \"What's the real-world impact of this gap? Give me a horror scenario.\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Here's a real scenario we've seen:</p><ol><li>Dev team creates 30 tables in Dev Lakehouse over 2 months</li><li>They commit notebooks and pipelines to Git regularly — everything looks clean</li><li>Release day: they deploy to UAT via Deployment Pipeline</li><li>UAT gets all the notebooks and pipelines — but <strong>ZERO</strong> tables</li><li>Every notebook fails immediately because the tables they reference don't exist</li><li>Team spends 3 days manually recreating tables in UAT by running notebooks one-by-one</li><li>They repeat this process for Prod</li><li>A month later, someone adds a column in Dev but forgets to add it in Prod</li><li>Production pipeline breaks on a Saturday at 2 AM</li></ol><p>Schema-as-Code prevents <strong>all</strong> of this.</p>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    5: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q15\",\n            \"question\": \"How do you handle rollbacks? What if a migration breaks something?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Multiple strategies:</p><ol><li><strong>Delta Lake Time Travel:</strong> Before running migrations, note the current table version. If something goes wrong, use <code>RESTORE TABLE table_name TO VERSION AS OF X</code> to rollback.</li><li><strong>Down migrations:</strong> Write a corresponding <em>undo</em> script for each migration (for example <code>v002_down.sql</code> that drops the tables or columns added by <code>v002</code>). The migration runner can support a <code>rollback</code> mode.</li><li><strong>Git revert:</strong> Revert the migration commit in Git and redeploy. The migration runner sees the state hasn't changed and does nothing (or runs the down migration if implemented).</li><li><strong>Forward-fix:</strong> Often the fastest approach — write a <code>v003</code> migration that fixes whatever <code>v002</code> broke, and deploy that.</li></ol><p>Best practice: always test migrations in Dev first, then UAT, with automated data quality checks after each step.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q16\",\n            \"question\": \"What about data migrations? Not just schema but actual data movement?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Schema-as-Code handles structure. For data:</p><ul><li><strong>Seed data:</strong> Include <code>INSERT</code> statements or PySpark data generation in your migration scripts for reference or lookup tables</li><li><strong>Data backfill:</strong> When adding a new column, include an <code>UPDATE</code> statement to populate existing rows</li><li><strong>Large data loads:</strong> Use separate Data Pipelines (which <strong>ARE</strong> tracked by Git) for ETL/ELT</li><li><strong>Data masking for Dev/UAT:</strong> Separate concern — use Data Pipelines with masking transformations</li></ul><p>The migration runner can handle both DDL and DML, but keep large data loads in dedicated pipelines.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q17\",\n            \"question\": \"How do you handle dependencies between tables? What if table B has a foreign key on table A?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Migration scripts run in version order (<code>v001</code>, <code>v002</code>, <code>v003</code>...). Within each script, order your <code>CREATE TABLE</code> statements so that parent tables are created before child tables.</p><p>Delta Lake in Fabric doesn't enforce foreign keys at the engine level (it's not like SQL Server), but maintaining the logical order is still important for:</p><ul><li>Data quality checks that validate referential integrity</li><li>Downstream notebooks and reports that expect certain tables to exist</li><li>Documentation and team understanding of the data model</li></ul>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q18\",\n            \"question\": \"Can we unit test our migration scripts before deploying?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Yes. The CI/CD pipeline should include a validation stage:</p><ol><li><strong>Syntax check:</strong> Parse the SQL or PySpark scripts for syntax errors before deployment</li><li><strong>Dry-run in Dev:</strong> Apply migrations to Dev first and run data quality checks</li><li><strong>Schema comparison:</strong> After migration, compare the resulting schema against an expected schema definition</li><li><strong>Automated testing notebook:</strong> The <code>data_quality_checks.py</code> notebook validates table existence, column types, row counts, and null checks</li></ol><p>This is what the <strong>Validate</strong> stage in our Azure DevOps pipeline does.</p>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    6: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q19\",\n            \"question\": \"What are the Fabric REST API rate limits? Will our automation hit throttling?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Fabric REST APIs have throttling limits:</p><ul><li>Most endpoints: 200 requests per minute per principal</li><li>Long-running operations (notebook execution): use the polling pattern with the <code>Location</code> header</li><li>Best practices: implement exponential backoff, use batch operations where available, cache workspace and item IDs</li></ul><p>For a typical CI/CD pipeline running 3-4 API calls per deployment, throttling is rarely an issue. It becomes relevant if you're running migrations against 50+ workspaces simultaneously.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q20\",\n            \"question\": \"How do you handle authentication securely? We can't have secrets in pipelines.\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Use managed identities or service principals with Azure Key Vault:</p><ol><li><strong>Service Principal:</strong> Register in Entra ID, store client secret in Azure Key Vault</li><li><strong>Pipeline integration:</strong> Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions retrieve the secret from Key Vault at runtime — never hardcoded</li><li><strong>Fabric permissions:</strong> Grant the service principal <strong>Contributor</strong> role on each workspace</li><li><strong>Token scoping:</strong> Request tokens scoped to <code>https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/.default</code></li><li><strong>Rotation:</strong> Automate secret rotation via Key Vault policies</li></ol><p>Alternative: use Workload Identity Federation (OIDC) with GitHub Actions — no secrets needed at all, just a trust relationship between GitHub and Entra ID.</p>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q21\",\n            \"question\": \"Can we monitor migration status and get alerts on failures?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>Yes, build observability into the pipeline:</p><ol><li><strong>Pipeline notifications:</strong> Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions can send emails, Teams messages, or Slack alerts on failure</li><li><strong>Migration history table:</strong> The <code>_migration_history</code> Delta table records every migration with timestamp, status, and duration</li><li><strong>Azure Monitor:</strong> Send custom metrics or logs to Azure Monitor for centralized alerting</li><li><strong>Dashboard:</strong> Build a Power BI report on top of the migration history tables across all environments — see migration status at a glance</li></ol>\"\n        }\n    ],\n    7: [\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q22\",\n            \"question\": \"If you had to recommend ONE approach — Lakehouse or Warehouse — for our CI/CD scenario, which would it be?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>It depends on your workload:</p><ul><li>If your primary need is <strong>BI reporting</strong> with full SQL support and native Git schema tracking → <strong>Warehouse</strong></li><li>If you need <strong>data engineering</strong> with Spark, semi-structured data, ML, and Direct Lake mode for Power BI → <strong>Lakehouse</strong> (with Schema-as-Code for CI/CD)</li><li><strong>Best practice:</strong> use <strong>BOTH</strong> in a medallion architecture — Lakehouse for Bronze/Silver (data engineering), Warehouse for Gold (reporting/serving). This gives you the best of both worlds.</li></ul>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q23\",\n            \"question\": \"How long would it take us to implement this Schema-as-Code approach in our environment?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>For a team that already has Fabric workspaces and a Git repo:</p><ul><li>Setting up migration scripts for existing tables: 1-2 days (extract current DDL, organize into versioned files)</li><li>Building the migration runner notebook: already done — use the one we showed today</li><li>Setting up the CI/CD pipeline: 1 day (adapt our Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions template)</li><li>Testing end-to-end: 1-2 days</li><li><strong>Total:</strong> about 1 week for initial setup, then it's just maintaining migration scripts as part of normal development</li></ul>\"\n        },\n        {\n            \"id\": \"Q24\",\n            \"question\": \"What about the fabric-cicd Python library? Should we use that instead?\",\n            \"answerHtml\": \"<p>The <code>fabric-cicd</code> Python library (open source from Microsoft) is great for deploying Fabric items programmatically. It handles item serialization, deployment, and parameterization.</p><p>However, it doesn't solve the Lakehouse schema gap — it deploys artifact definitions, not table schemas. You can use it alongside Schema-as-Code: <code>fabric-cicd</code> for deploying notebooks or pipelines + Schema-as-Code for deploying table schemas. They complement each other.</p>\"\n        }\n    ]\n}\n\ntemplate = r'''<!DOCTYPE html>\n<html lang=\"en\">\n<head>\n<meta charset=\"UTF-8\">\n<meta name=\"viewport\" content=\"width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0\">\n<title>Fabric CI/CD Demo Guide — Digital Realty</title>\n<style>\n  :root {\n    --bg: #0d1117;\n    --surface: #161b22;\n    --surface-2: #1f2630;\n    --surface-3: #212936;\n    --border: #30363d;\n    --text: #e6edf3;\n    --muted: #8b949e;\n    --accent: #58a6ff;\n    --accent-2: #3fb950;\n    --purple: #bc8cff;\n    --warning: #d29922;\n    --danger: #f85149;\n    --danger-soft: #ff7b72;\n    --orange: #f0883e;\n    --note-bg: #111a2a;\n    --note-border: #2d4a7a;\n    --tip-bg: #122117;\n    --tip-border: #2d6a3d;\n    --qa-bg: rgba(240, 136, 62, 0.08);\n    --qa-border: rgba(240, 136, 62, 0.45);\n    --shadow: 0 18px 40px rgba(0,0,0,.35);\n  }\n\n  * { box-sizing: border-box; }\n  html { scroll-behavior: smooth; }\n  body {\n    margin: 0;\n    font-family: 'Segoe UI', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, sans-serif;\n    background: var(--bg);\n    color: var(--text);\n    line-height: 1.6;\n  }\n  button, input, textarea, select { font: inherit; }\n  code, pre, kbd { font-family: 'Cascadia Code', Consolas, monospace; }\n  a { color: inherit; }\n\n  .topbar {\n    position: fixed;\n    top: 0;\n    left: 0;\n    right: 0;\n    height: 64px;\n    z-index: 120;\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 16px;\n    padding: 0 24px;\n    background: rgba(22, 27, 34, 0.96);\n    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);\n    backdrop-filter: blur(14px);\n  }\n  .logo-block {\n    display: flex;\n    flex-direction: column;\n    gap: 2px;\n    min-width: 225px;\n  }\n  .logo {\n    font-weight: 700;\n    color: var(--accent);\n    letter-spacing: 0.2px;\n  }\n  .logo span { color: var(--text); }\n  .tagline {\n    color: var(--muted);\n    font-size: 12px;\n  }\n  .timer-pill {\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 12px;\n    padding: 8px 14px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: rgba(13, 17, 23, 0.66);\n  }\n  .timer {\n    min-width: 72px;\n    text-align: center;\n    font-size: 18px;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    color: var(--accent-2);\n  }\n  .topbar .controls {\n    margin-left: auto;\n    display: flex;\n    flex-wrap: wrap;\n    gap: 8px;\n    justify-content: flex-end;\n  }\n  .btn {\n    appearance: none;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: var(--surface-2);\n    color: var(--text);\n    padding: 8px 14px;\n    border-radius: 8px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    transition: 0.15s ease;\n    box-shadow: none;\n  }\n  .btn:hover {\n    transform: translateY(-1px);\n    border-color: #4b5563;\n    background: #262f3d;\n  }\n  .btn.active {\n    background: rgba(88, 166, 255, 0.18);\n    border-color: rgba(88, 166, 255, 0.5);\n    color: #dbeafe;\n  }\n  .btn.warn.active {\n    background: rgba(240, 136, 62, 0.16);\n    border-color: rgba(240, 136, 62, 0.55);\n    color: #ffddb9;\n  }\n  .btn.primary {\n    background: rgba(63, 185, 80, 0.14);\n    border-color: rgba(63, 185, 80, 0.45);\n  }\n\n  .progress-track {\n    position: fixed;\n    top: 64px;\n    left: 0;\n    right: 0;\n    height: 4px;\n    z-index: 119;\n    background: rgba(48, 54, 61, 0.85);\n  }\n  .progress-fill {\n    height: 100%;\n    width: 0;\n    background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent), var(--accent-2));\n    transition: width .3s ease;\n  }\n\n  .sidebar {\n    position: fixed;\n    top: 68px;\n    left: 0;\n    bottom: 0;\n    width: 300px;\n    padding: 16px 0 24px;\n    overflow-y: auto;\n    background: var(--surface);\n    border-right: 1px solid var(--border);\n  }\n  .sidebar-summary {\n    margin: 0 16px 16px;\n    padding: 16px;\n    border-radius: 14px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(88,166,255,0.12), rgba(88,166,255,0.04));\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow);\n  }\n  .sidebar-summary h1 {\n    margin: 0 0 6px;\n    font-size: 16px;\n  }\n  .sidebar-summary p {\n    margin: 0 0 12px;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .summary-grid {\n    display: grid;\n    grid-template-columns: repeat(2, minmax(0, 1fr));\n    gap: 8px;\n  }\n  .summary-card {\n    padding: 10px 12px;\n    border-radius: 10px;\n    background: rgba(13, 17, 23, 0.55);\n    border: 1px solid rgba(88,166,255,0.1);\n  }\n  .summary-card .label {\n    display: block;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    letter-spacing: .08em;\n    margin-bottom: 4px;\n  }\n  .summary-card strong { font-size: 14px; }\n  .sidebar h3 {\n    margin: 18px 0 8px;\n    padding: 0 20px;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    letter-spacing: .16em;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n  }\n  .sidebar a, .sidebar button.linklike {\n    width: 100%;\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 10px;\n    border: 0;\n    border-left: 3px solid transparent;\n    background: transparent;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    text-decoration: none;\n    text-align: left;\n    padding: 8px 20px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    transition: 0.15s ease;\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .sidebar a:hover, .sidebar button.linklike:hover {\n    background: var(--surface-2);\n    color: var(--text);\n  }\n  .sidebar a.active {\n    color: var(--accent);\n    background: rgba(88, 166, 255, 0.08);\n    border-left-color: var(--accent);\n    font-weight: 600;\n  }\n  .sidebar .num {\n    min-width: 42px;\n    display: inline-flex;\n    justify-content: center;\n    padding: 2px 8px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    background: rgba(48,54,61,0.9);\n    color: var(--text);\n    font-size: 11px;\n    font-weight: 700;\n  }\n  .sidebar .check { color: var(--accent-2); }\n  .sidebar .meta-link { padding-left: 36px; }\n  .sidebar .kb-link::before { content: '📚'; }\n  .sidebar .qa-link::before { content: '❓'; }\n\n  main {\n    margin-left: 300px;\n    padding: 100px 40px 100px;\n    max-width: 1180px;\n  }\n  .links-bar {\n    display: flex;\n    flex-wrap: wrap;\n    gap: 10px;\n    margin-bottom: 24px;\n  }\n  .links-bar a {\n    padding: 8px 14px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    text-decoration: none;\n    font-size: 13px;\n    color: var(--accent);\n    background: rgba(31,38,48,0.8);\n  }\n  .links-bar a:hover { background: #253043; }\n\n  .part-header {\n    margin: 0 0 22px;\n    padding: 24px 26px;\n    border-radius: 18px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(88,166,255,0.1), rgba(22,27,34,1));\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow);\n  }\n  .part-header + .step { margin-top: 0; }\n  .part-num {\n    color: var(--accent);\n    font-size: 12px;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    letter-spacing: .18em;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    margin-bottom: 6px;\n  }\n  .part-header h2 {\n    margin: 0 0 10px;\n    font-size: 28px;\n  }\n  .part-meta {\n    display: flex;\n    flex-wrap: wrap;\n    gap: 10px;\n    margin-bottom: 14px;\n  }\n  .pill {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 6px;\n    padding: 4px 10px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    font-size: 12px;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08);\n    background: rgba(13,17,23,0.45);\n    color: var(--text);\n  }\n  .goal {\n    padding: 12px 14px;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    background: rgba(13,17,23,0.5);\n    border-left: 4px solid var(--accent);\n    color: #d0d7de;\n    font-size: 14px;\n  }\n\n  .step {\n    margin-bottom: 18px;\n    border-radius: 16px;\n    background: var(--surface);\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    overflow: hidden;\n    box-shadow: 0 8px 26px rgba(0,0,0,0.18);\n  }\n  .step.current {\n    border-color: rgba(88,166,255,0.7);\n    box-shadow: 0 0 0 1px rgba(88,166,255,0.35), 0 10px 28px rgba(0,0,0,0.25);\n  }\n  .step.done { opacity: 0.92; }\n  .step.done .step-num {\n    background: var(--accent-2);\n    color: #04160a;\n  }\n  .step-head {\n    display: grid;\n    grid-template-columns: auto 1fr auto auto;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 14px;\n    padding: 16px 20px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    transition: background .15s ease;\n  }\n  .step-head:hover { background: rgba(88,166,255,0.04); }\n  .step-num {\n    width: 42px;\n    height: 42px;\n    border-radius: 50%;\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: center;\n    background: var(--accent);\n    color: #081018;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    font-size: 14px;\n    box-shadow: inset 0 -1px 0 rgba(0,0,0,0.25);\n  }\n  .step-title-block {\n    min-width: 0;\n  }\n  .step-title {\n    font-size: 16px;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    margin-bottom: 2px;\n  }\n  .step-subtitle {\n    color: var(--muted);\n    font-size: 12px;\n  }\n  .step-screen {\n    font-size: 12px;\n    color: #f5d0fe;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(188,140,255,0.3);\n    padding: 4px 8px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    background: rgba(188,140,255,0.09);\n  }\n  .step-status {\n    font-size: 18px;\n  }\n  .step-body {\n    padding: 0 20px 20px;\n  }\n  .step-body.collapsed { display: none; }\n\n  .action {\n    display: flex;\n    gap: 12px;\n    align-items: flex-start;\n    padding: 10px 0;\n    border-top: 1px solid rgba(48,54,61,0.6);\n  }\n  .action:first-child { border-top: 0; }\n  .action-check {\n    width: 22px;\n    height: 22px;\n    margin-top: 2px;\n    border-radius: 6px;\n    border: 2px solid #4b5563;\n    background: transparent;\n    flex-shrink: 0;\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: center;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    transition: 0.15s ease;\n  }\n  .action-check:hover { border-color: var(--accent); }\n  .action-check.checked {\n    background: var(--accent-2);\n    border-color: var(--accent-2);\n  }\n  .action-check.checked::after {\n    content: '✓';\n    color: #06180b;\n    font-weight: 900;\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .action-do {\n    color: var(--text);\n    font-size: 14px;\n  }\n  .action-do strong { color: var(--accent); }\n  .action-do code {\n    background: rgba(110,118,129,0.2);\n    color: #ffd38a;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(110,118,129,0.25);\n    padding: 1px 6px;\n    border-radius: 6px;\n  }\n  pre {\n    margin: 10px 0 2px;\n    padding: 14px 16px;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    overflow-x: auto;\n    background: #0a0f16;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(48,54,61,0.9);\n    color: #dbe5f0;\n    font-size: 13px;\n    line-height: 1.5;\n  }\n\n  .tip, .notes {\n    margin-top: 12px;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    padding: 14px 16px;\n  }\n  .tip {\n    border: 1px solid var(--tip-border);\n    background: var(--tip-bg);\n    color: #c8f5d2;\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .tip::before {\n    content: '💡 ';\n    font-size: 14px;\n  }\n  .notes {\n    border: 1px solid var(--note-border);\n    background: var(--note-bg);\n    color: #c8d6ea;\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .notes.hidden { display: none; }\n  .notes-label {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 6px;\n    margin-bottom: 8px;\n    color: var(--accent);\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    font-weight: 800;\n    letter-spacing: .12em;\n  }\n  .notes blockquote {\n    margin: 0;\n    padding: 10px 14px;\n    border-left: 4px solid rgba(88,166,255,0.75);\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.06);\n    border-radius: 0 10px 10px 0;\n    color: var(--text);\n    font-style: italic;\n  }\n\n  .qa-section {\n    margin: 18px 0 40px;\n    border-radius: 18px;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(240,136,62,0.28);\n    background: rgba(22,27,34,0.82);\n    overflow: hidden;\n  }\n  .qa-section.hidden { display: none; }\n  .qa-toggle {\n    width: 100%;\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: space-between;\n    gap: 12px;\n    border: 0;\n    background: linear-gradient(90deg, rgba(240,136,62,0.16), rgba(248,81,73,0.08));\n    color: var(--text);\n    padding: 18px 20px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n  }\n  .qa-toggle:hover { filter: brightness(1.05); }\n  .qa-toggle-left {\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 12px;\n  }\n  .qa-toggle h3 {\n    margin: 0;\n    font-size: 17px;\n  }\n  .qa-toggle small {\n    color: #f6caa5;\n    display: block;\n    font-size: 12px;\n    margin-top: 2px;\n  }\n  .qa-count {\n    padding: 4px 10px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.12);\n    background: rgba(13,17,23,0.45);\n    color: #ffddb9;\n    font-size: 12px;\n  }\n  .qa-chevron {\n    font-size: 18px;\n    color: #ffb980;\n  }\n  .qa-panel {\n    padding: 18px;\n    display: grid;\n    gap: 14px;\n  }\n  .qa-panel.collapsed { display: none; }\n  .qa-card {\n    padding: 18px;\n    border-radius: 16px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--qa-border);\n    background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(248,81,73,0.06), rgba(240,136,62,0.04));\n    box-shadow: inset 4px 0 0 rgba(240,136,62,0.8);\n  }\n  .qa-card-head {\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: flex-start;\n    justify-content: space-between;\n    gap: 12px;\n    margin-bottom: 12px;\n  }\n  .qa-label {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 8px;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    letter-spacing: .14em;\n    color: #ffceb4;\n  }\n  .qa-badges {\n    display: flex;\n    flex-wrap: wrap;\n    gap: 8px;\n  }\n  .badge {\n    padding: 4px 8px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    letter-spacing: .08em;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.12);\n  }\n  .badge.demo {\n    background: rgba(240,136,62,0.18);\n    border-color: rgba(240,136,62,0.4);\n    color: #ffd7b1;\n  }\n  .badge.followup {\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.15);\n    border-color: rgba(88,166,255,0.35);\n    color: #dbeafe;\n  }\n  .qa-question {\n    font-size: 18px;\n    line-height: 1.45;\n    font-weight: 800;\n    color: var(--danger-soft);\n    margin: 0;\n  }\n  .qa-answer-grid {\n    display: grid;\n    gap: 12px;\n  }\n  .qa-block {\n    padding: 14px 16px;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    background: rgba(13,17,23,0.44);\n    border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);\n  }\n  .qa-block.follow-up {\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.08);\n    border-color: rgba(88,166,255,0.2);\n  }\n  .qa-block.demo {\n    background: rgba(240,136,62,0.1);\n    border-color: rgba(240,136,62,0.28);\n  }\n  .qa-block-title {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 6px;\n    margin-bottom: 8px;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    letter-spacing: .12em;\n    font-weight: 800;\n    color: #ffcca4;\n  }\n  .qa-block.follow-up .qa-block-title { color: #bfdbfe; }\n  .qa-block p:first-child, .qa-block ul:first-child, .qa-block ol:first-child { margin-top: 0; }\n  .qa-block p:last-child, .qa-block ul:last-child, .qa-block ol:last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }\n  .qa-block ul, .qa-block ol { padding-left: 20px; }\n\n  .modal {\n    position: fixed;\n    inset: 0;\n    z-index: 250;\n    display: none;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: center;\n    padding: 28px;\n    background: rgba(0,0,0,0.72);\n    backdrop-filter: blur(10px);\n  }\n  .modal.show { display: flex; }\n  .modal-shell {\n    width: min(1120px, 100%);\n    max-height: min(88vh, 980px);\n    overflow: hidden;\n    border-radius: 20px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: var(--surface);\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow);\n  }\n  .modal-header {\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: space-between;\n    gap: 16px;\n    padding: 18px 22px;\n    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: linear-gradient(90deg, rgba(88,166,255,0.08), rgba(22,27,34,1));\n  }\n  .modal-header h3 {\n    margin: 0;\n    font-size: 20px;\n  }\n  .modal-header p {\n    margin: 4px 0 0;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n  .modal-body { overflow: auto; max-height: calc(88vh - 78px); }\n\n  .knowledge-layout {\n    display: grid;\n    grid-template-columns: 290px 1fr;\n    min-height: 580px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav {\n    padding: 16px;\n    border-right: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: rgba(13,17,23,0.55);\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav button {\n    width: 100%;\n    text-align: left;\n    background: transparent;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    border: 1px solid transparent;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    padding: 12px 14px;\n    margin-bottom: 8px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    transition: 0.15s ease;\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav button:hover {\n    color: var(--text);\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.06);\n    border-color: rgba(88,166,255,0.12);\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav button.active {\n    color: var(--text);\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.12);\n    border-color: rgba(88,166,255,0.28);\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav .kb-nav-title {\n    display: block;\n    font-weight: 700;\n    margin-bottom: 4px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-nav .kb-nav-summary {\n    display: block;\n    font-size: 12px;\n    color: var(--muted);\n    line-height: 1.5;\n  }\n  .knowledge-content {\n    padding: 22px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-card {\n    border-radius: 18px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(88,166,255,0.08), rgba(13,17,23,0.25));\n    padding: 22px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-kicker {\n    color: var(--accent);\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    font-size: 11px;\n    letter-spacing: .14em;\n    font-weight: 800;\n    margin-bottom: 8px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-card h4 {\n    margin: 0 0 8px;\n    font-size: 26px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-summary {\n    margin: 0 0 18px;\n    font-size: 15px;\n    color: #d0d7de;\n  }\n  .knowledge-body {\n    display: grid;\n    gap: 14px;\n  }\n  .knowledge-body p,\n  .knowledge-body ul,\n  .knowledge-body ol,\n  .knowledge-body table,\n  .knowledge-body .knowledge-callout { margin: 0; }\n  .knowledge-body ul, .knowledge-body ol { padding-left: 20px; }\n  .knowledge-body table {\n    width: 100%;\n    border-collapse: collapse;\n    overflow: hidden;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n  }\n  .knowledge-body th,\n  .knowledge-body td {\n    padding: 12px 14px;\n    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);\n    border-right: 1px solid rgba(48,54,61,0.7);\n    vertical-align: top;\n  }\n  .knowledge-body th:last-child,\n  .knowledge-body td:last-child { border-right: 0; }\n  .knowledge-body th {\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.12);\n    text-align: left;\n  }\n  .knowledge-callout {\n    padding: 14px 16px;\n    border-radius: 12px;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(88,166,255,0.24);\n    background: rgba(88,166,255,0.08);\n  }\n\n  .shortcuts-grid {\n    padding: 20px 22px 22px;\n    display: grid;\n    gap: 10px;\n  }\n  .shortcut-row {\n    display: flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: space-between;\n    gap: 18px;\n    padding: 10px 0;\n    border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(48,54,61,0.65);\n  }\n  .shortcut-row:last-child { border-bottom: 0; }\n  kbd {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    justify-content: center;\n    min-width: 32px;\n    padding: 4px 10px;\n    border-radius: 8px;\n    border: 1px solid var(--border);\n    background: var(--surface-2);\n    font-size: 13px;\n  }\n\n  .kb-fab {\n    position: fixed;\n    right: 24px;\n    bottom: 24px;\n    z-index: 110;\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 10px;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(88,166,255,0.32);\n    background: rgba(22,27,34,0.94);\n    color: var(--text);\n    padding: 12px 16px;\n    border-radius: 999px;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow);\n  }\n  .kb-fab:hover { background: #1d2430; }\n\n  .hidden { display: none !important; }\n\n  @media (max-width: 1080px) {\n    .topbar {\n      height: auto;\n      min-height: 64px;\n      flex-wrap: wrap;\n      padding: 12px 16px;\n    }\n    .progress-track { top: 88px; }\n    .sidebar { top: 92px; }\n    main { padding-top: 126px; }\n  }\n  @media (max-width: 980px) {\n    .sidebar { display: none; }\n    main { margin-left: 0; padding: 110px 18px 88px; }\n    .knowledge-layout { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }\n    .knowledge-nav { border-right: 0; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border); }\n  }\n  @media (max-width: 760px) {\n    .topbar { gap: 10px; }\n    .topbar .controls { width: 100%; }\n    .step-head {\n      grid-template-columns: auto 1fr;\n      grid-template-areas:\n        'num title'\n        'screen status';\n    }\n    .step-num { grid-area: num; }\n    .step-title-block { grid-area: title; }\n    .step-screen { grid-area: screen; justify-self: start; }\n    .step-status { grid-area: status; justify-self: end; }\n    .kb-fab span:last-child { display: none; }\n  }\n  @media print {\n    .topbar, .progress-track, .sidebar, .kb-fab, .modal { display: none !important; }\n    main { margin: 0; padding: 24px; }\n    .notes.hidden, .qa-section.hidden { display: block !important; }\n    .qa-panel.collapsed { display: grid !important; }\n  }\n</style>\n</head>\n<body>\n  <div class=\"topbar\">\n    <div class=\"logo-block\">\n      <div class=\"logo\">🚀 Fabric CI/CD Demo <span>— Digital Realty</span></div>\n      <div class=\"tagline\">Second-monitor guide for the 60-minute live presentation</div>\n    </div>\n    <div class=\"timer-pill\">\n      <div class=\"timer\" id=\"timer\">00:00</div>\n      <button class=\"btn primary\" id=\"timerBtn\" onclick=\"toggleTimer()\">▶ Start Timer</button>\n    </div>\n    <div class=\"controls\">\n      <button class=\"btn active\" id=\"notesBtn\" onclick=\"toggleNotes()\">📝 Notes ON</button>\n      <button class=\"btn warn active\" id=\"qaBtn\" onclick=\"toggleQA()\">❓ Q&amp;A ON</button>\n      <button class=\"btn\" onclick=\"toggleShortcuts(true)\">⌨ Shortcuts</button>\n      <button class=\"btn\" onclick=\"resetAll()\">↺ Reset</button>\n    </div>\n  </div>\n\n  <div class=\"progress-track\"><div class=\"progress-fill\" id=\"progressFill\"></div></div>\n\n  <nav class=\"sidebar\" id=\"sidebar\"></nav>\n  <main id=\"main\"></main>\n\n  <button class=\"kb-fab\" onclick=\"openKnowledgeBase()\" title=\"Open knowledge base\">\n    <span>📚</span>\n    <span>Knowledge Base</span>\n  </button>\n\n  <div class=\"modal\" id=\"knowledgeModal\" onclick=\"if (event.target === this) closeKnowledgeBase()\">\n    <div class=\"modal-shell\">\n      <div class=\"modal-header\">\n        <div>\n          <h3>📚 Fabric Knowledge Base</h3>\n          <p>Rapid concept explainers for customer questions and schema-gap conversations.</p>\n        </div>\n        <button class=\"btn\" onclick=\"closeKnowledgeBase()\">Close</button>\n      </div>\n      <div class=\"modal-body\">\n        <div class=\"knowledge-layout\">\n          <div class=\"knowledge-nav\" id=\"knowledgeNav\"></div>\n          <div class=\"knowledge-content\" id=\"knowledgeContent\"></div>\n        </div>\n      </div>\n    </div>\n  </div>\n\n  <div class=\"modal\" id=\"shortcutsModal\" onclick=\"if (event.target === this) toggleShortcuts(false)\">\n    <div class=\"modal-shell\" style=\"width:min(560px,100%);\">\n      <div class=\"modal-header\">\n        <div>\n          <h3>⌨ Keyboard Shortcuts</h3>\n          <p>Designed for fast presenter control without touching the mouse.</p>\n        </div>\n        <button class=\"btn\" onclick=\"toggleShortcuts(false)\">Close</button>\n      </div>\n      <div class=\"modal-body\">\n        <div class=\"shortcuts-grid\">\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Next step</span><span><kbd>→</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Previous step</span><span><kbd>←</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Toggle notes</span><span><kbd>H</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Toggle Q&amp;A sections</span><span><kbd>Q</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Start / pause timer</span><span><kbd>T</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Mark current step done</span><span><kbd>D</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Open knowledge base</span><span><kbd>K</kbd></span></div>\n          <div class=\"shortcut-row\"><span>Close modals</span><span><kbd>Esc</kbd></span></div>\n        </div>\n      </div>\n    </div>\n  </div>\n\n<script>\nconst STORAGE_KEY = 'fabric-demo-guide-v2';\nconst KNOWLEDGE_BASE = __KB_DATA__;\nconst QA_BY_PART = __QA_DATA__;\nconst DEMO = __DEMO_DATA__;\n\nconst allSteps = [];\nDEMO.forEach(part => part.steps.forEach(step => allSteps.push({ ...step, part: part.part, partTitle: part.partTitle })));\nconst totalSteps = allSteps.length;\nconst stepIndexMap = Object.fromEntries(allSteps.map((step, idx) => [step.id, idx]));\n\nlet notesVisible = true;\nlet qasVisible = true;\nlet timerRunning = false;\nlet timerSeconds = 0;\nlet timerInterval = null;\nlet currentStepIdx = 0;\nlet completedSteps = new Set();\nlet checkedActions = {};\nlet partQaOpen = Object.fromEntries(DEMO.map(part => [part.part, false]));\nlet selectedKnowledgeId = KNOWLEDGE_BASE[0]?.id || null;\n\nfunction safeSaveState() {\n  try {\n    localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, JSON.stringify({\n      notesVisible,\n      qasVisible,\n      timerSeconds,\n      currentStepIdx,\n      completedSteps: Array.from(completedSteps),\n      checkedActions,\n      partQaOpen,\n      selectedKnowledgeId\n    }));\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.warn('State save skipped', err);\n  }\n}\n\nfunction loadState() {\n  try {\n    const raw = localStorage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY);\n    if (!raw) return;\n    const state = JSON.parse(raw);\n    notesVisible = state.notesVisible ?? true;\n    qasVisible = state.qasVisible ?? true;\n    timerSeconds = Number.isFinite(state.timerSeconds) ? state.timerSeconds : 0;\n    currentStepIdx = Math.max(0, Math.min(totalSteps - 1, state.currentStepIdx ?? 0));\n    completedSteps = new Set(Array.isArray(state.completedSteps) ? state.completedSteps : []);\n    checkedActions = state.checkedActions && typeof state.checkedActions === 'object' ? state.checkedActions : {};\n    partQaOpen = { ...partQaOpen, ...(state.partQaOpen || {}) };\n    selectedKnowledgeId = state.selectedKnowledgeId || selectedKnowledgeId;\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.warn('State load skipped', err);\n  }\n}\n\nfunction render() {\n  renderSidebar();\n  renderMain();\n  renderKnowledgeBase();\n  updateTimerDisplay();\n  updateProgress();\n  updateToggleButtons();\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction renderSidebar() {\n  const sidebar = document.getElementById('sidebar');\n  const qaCount = Object.values(QA_BY_PART).reduce((sum, items) => sum + items.length, 0);\n  let html = `\n    <div class=\"sidebar-summary\">\n      <h1>Live Demo Runbook</h1>\n      <p>Every step, speaker note, concept refresher, and tough customer question in one place.</p>\n      <div class=\"summary-grid\">\n        <div class=\"summary-card\"><span class=\"label\">Progress</span><strong id=\"progressLabel\">${completedSteps.size}/${totalSteps} steps</strong></div>\n        <div class=\"summary-card\"><span class=\"label\">Hard Q&amp;A</span><strong>${qaCount} answers</strong></div>\n        <div class=\"summary-card\"><span class=\"label\">Knowledge Base</span><strong>${KNOWLEDGE_BASE.length} concepts</strong></div>\n        <div class=\"summary-card\"><span class=\"label\">Timer Goal</span><strong>60 minutes</strong></div>\n      </div>\n    </div>\n    <h3>Knowledge Base</h3>\n  `;\n\n  KNOWLEDGE_BASE.forEach(item => {\n    html += `<button class=\"linklike kb-link\" onclick=\"openKnowledgeBase('${item.id}')\">${item.title}</button>`;\n  });\n\n  DEMO.forEach(part => {\n    html += `<h3>Part ${part.part}: ${part.partTitle}</h3>`;\n    part.steps.forEach(step => {\n      const idx = stepIndexMap[step.id];\n      const active = idx === currentStepIdx ? 'active' : '';\n      const done = completedSteps.has(step.id) ? '<span class=\"check\">✓</span>' : '';\n      html += `<a href=\"#step-${step.id}\" class=\"${active}\" onclick=\"goToStep(${idx}); return false;\"><span class=\"num\">${step.id}</span><span>${step.title}</span>${done}</a>`;\n    });\n    html += `<a href=\"#part-qa-${part.part}\" class=\"meta-link qa-link\" onclick=\"goToPartQA(${part.part}); return false;\">Tough Questions</a>`;\n  });\n\n  sidebar.innerHTML = html;\n}\n\nfunction renderMain() {\n  const main = document.getElementById('main');\n  let html = `\n    <div class=\"links-bar\">\n      <a href=\"https://app.fabric.microsoft.com\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer\">🌐 Fabric Portal</a>\n      <a href=\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/fabric/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer\">📘 Fabric REST API</a>\n      <a href=\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/git-integration/git-automation\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer\">📘 Git Automation</a>\n      <a href=\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/deployment-pipelines/pipeline-automation-fabric\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer\">📘 Pipeline API</a>\n    </div>\n  `;\n\n  DEMO.forEach(part => {\n    const questions = QA_BY_PART[part.part] || [];\n    html += `\n      <section class=\"part-header\" id=\"part-${part.part}\">\n        <div class=\"part-num\">Part ${part.part} of ${DEMO.length}</div>\n        <h2>${part.partTitle}</h2>\n        <div class=\"part-meta\">\n          <span class=\"pill\">⏱ ${part.duration}</span>\n          <span class=\"pill\">🧭 ${part.steps.length} steps</span>\n          <span class=\"pill\">❓ ${questions.length} tough questions</span>\n        </div>\n        <div class=\"goal\">🎯 ${part.goal}</div>\n      </section>\n    `;\n\n    part.steps.forEach(step => {\n      const idx = stepIndexMap[step.id];\n      const isCurrent = idx === currentStepIdx;\n      const isDone = completedSteps.has(step.id);\n      const stepClass = isCurrent ? 'current' : (isDone ? 'done' : '');\n      const bodyClass = (isCurrent || isDone) ? '' : 'collapsed';\n      const statusIcon = isDone ? '✅' : (isCurrent ? '👉' : '○');\n      html += `\n        <article class=\"step ${stepClass}\" id=\"step-${step.id}\">\n          <div class=\"step-head\" onclick=\"goToStep(${idx})\">\n            <div class=\"step-num\">${step.id}</div>\n            <div class=\"step-title-block\">\n              <div class=\"step-title\">${step.title}</div>\n              <div class=\"step-subtitle\">Part ${part.part} · ${part.partTitle}</div>\n            </div>\n            <div class=\"step-screen\">${step.screen}</div>\n            <div class=\"step-status\">${statusIcon}</div>\n          </div>\n          <div class=\"step-body ${bodyClass}\">\n      `;\n\n      step.actions.forEach((action, actionIdx) => {\n        const checked = checkedActions[`${step.id}:${actionIdx}`] ? 'checked' : '';\n        html += `\n          <div class=\"action\">\n            <div class=\"action-check ${checked}\" onclick=\"toggleAction(event, '${step.id}', ${actionIdx})\"></div>\n            <div class=\"action-do\">${action}</div>\n          </div>\n        `;\n      });\n\n      if (step.tips && step.tips.length) {\n        step.tips.forEach(tip => {\n          html += `<div class=\"tip\">${tip}</div>`;\n        });\n      }\n\n      if (step.notes) {\n        html += `\n          <div class=\"notes ${notesVisible ? '' : 'hidden'}\">\n            <div class=\"notes-label\">🎤 What to say</div>\n            ${step.notes}\n          </div>\n        `;\n      }\n\n      html += `</div></article>`;\n    });\n\n    html += renderQaSection(part, questions);\n  });\n\n  main.innerHTML = html;\n}\n\nfunction renderQaSection(part, questions) {\n  const expanded = !!partQaOpen[part.part];\n  const sectionClass = qasVisible ? 'qa-section' : 'qa-section hidden';\n  return `\n    <section class=\"${sectionClass}\" id=\"part-qa-${part.part}\">\n      <button class=\"qa-toggle\" onclick=\"togglePartQa(${part.part})\">\n        <div class=\"qa-toggle-left\">\n          <div>\n            <h3>❓ Tough Questions</h3>\n            <small>Hard objections and structured answers for ${part.partTitle}</small>\n          </div>\n          <span class=\"qa-count\">${questions.length} ready answers</span>\n        </div>\n        <div class=\"qa-chevron\">${expanded ? '▾' : '▸'}</div>\n      </button>\n      <div class=\"qa-panel ${expanded ? '' : 'collapsed'}\">\n        ${questions.map(renderQaCard).join('')}\n      </div>\n    </section>\n  `;\n}\n\nfunction renderQaCard(question) {\n  const badges = [];\n  if (question.demoHtml) badges.push('<span class=\"badge demo\">Demo-worthy</span>');\n  if (question.followUpHtml) badges.push('<span class=\"badge followup\">Follow-up ready</span>');\n  return `\n    <article class=\"qa-card\">\n      <div class=\"qa-card-head\">\n        <div>\n          <div class=\"qa-label\">${question.id}</div>\n          <p class=\"qa-question\">${question.question}</p>\n        </div>\n        <div class=\"qa-badges\">${badges.join('')}</div>\n      </div>\n      <div class=\"qa-answer-grid\">\n        <div class=\"qa-block\">\n          <div class=\"qa-block-title\">Structured answer</div>\n          ${question.answerHtml}\n        </div>\n        ${question.impactHtml ? `<div class=\"qa-block\"><div class=\"qa-block-title\">Impact / why it matters</div>${question.impactHtml}</div>` : ''}\n        ${question.followUpHtml ? `<div class=\"qa-block follow-up\"><div class=\"qa-block-title\">Follow-up</div>${question.followUpHtml}</div>` : ''}\n        ${question.demoHtml ? `<div class=\"qa-block demo\"><div class=\"qa-block-title\">Demo-worthy</div>${question.demoHtml}</div>` : ''}\n      </div>\n    </article>\n  `;\n}\n\nfunction renderKnowledgeBase() {\n  const nav = document.getElementById('knowledgeNav');\n  const content = document.getElementById('knowledgeContent');\n  const current = KNOWLEDGE_BASE.find(item => item.id === selectedKnowledgeId) || KNOWLEDGE_BASE[0];\n  if (!current) return;\n\n  nav.innerHTML = KNOWLEDGE_BASE.map((item, idx) => `\n    <button class=\"${item.id === current.id ? 'active' : ''}\" onclick=\"switchKnowledge('${item.id}')\">\n      <span class=\"kb-nav-title\">${idx + 1}. ${item.title}</span>\n      <span class=\"kb-nav-summary\">${item.summary}</span>\n    </button>\n  `).join('');\n\n  const currentIndex = KNOWLEDGE_BASE.findIndex(item => item.id === current.id) + 1;\n  content.innerHTML = `\n    <div class=\"knowledge-card\">\n      <div class=\"knowledge-kicker\">Concept ${currentIndex} of ${KNOWLEDGE_BASE.length}</div>\n      <h4>${current.title}</h4>\n      <p class=\"knowledge-summary\">${current.summary}</p>\n      <div class=\"knowledge-body\">${current.contentHtml}</div>\n    </div>\n  `;\n}\n\nfunction updateProgress() {\n  const percent = totalSteps ? (completedSteps.size / totalSteps) * 100 : 0;\n  document.getElementById('progressFill').style.width = percent + '%';\n  const progressLabel = document.getElementById('progressLabel');\n  if (progressLabel) progressLabel.textContent = `${completedSteps.size}/${totalSteps} steps`;\n}\n\nfunction updateToggleButtons() {\n  const notesBtn = document.getElementById('notesBtn');\n  const qaBtn = document.getElementById('qaBtn');\n  notesBtn.textContent = notesVisible ? '📝 Notes ON' : '📝 Notes OFF';\n  qaBtn.textContent = qasVisible ? '❓ Q&A ON' : '❓ Q&A OFF';\n  notesBtn.classList.toggle('active', notesVisible);\n  qaBtn.classList.toggle('active', qasVisible);\n}\n\nfunction updateTimerDisplay() {\n  const timer = document.getElementById('timer');\n  const minutes = Math.floor(timerSeconds / 60).toString().padStart(2, '0');\n  const seconds = (timerSeconds % 60).toString().padStart(2, '0');\n  timer.textContent = `${minutes}:${seconds}`;\n  if (timerSeconds > 3300) {\n    timer.style.color = '#f85149';\n  } else if (timerSeconds > 2700) {\n    timer.style.color = '#d29922';\n  } else {\n    timer.style.color = '#3fb950';\n  }\n  document.getElementById('timerBtn').textContent = timerRunning ? '⏸ Pause' : (timerSeconds > 0 ? '▶ Resume' : '▶ Start Timer');\n}\n\nfunction goToStep(idx) {\n  if (idx < 0 || idx >= totalSteps) return;\n  currentStepIdx = idx;\n  render();\n  const el = document.getElementById(`step-${allSteps[idx].id}`);\n  if (el) el.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });\n}\n\nfunction nextStep() {\n  completedSteps.add(allSteps[currentStepIdx].id);\n  if (currentStepIdx < totalSteps - 1) {\n    goToStep(currentStepIdx + 1);\n  } else {\n    render();\n  }\n}\n\nfunction prevStep() {\n  if (currentStepIdx > 0) {\n    goToStep(currentStepIdx - 1);\n  }\n}\n\nfunction markDone() {\n  const step = allSteps[currentStepIdx];\n  if (completedSteps.has(step.id)) completedSteps.delete(step.id);\n  else completedSteps.add(step.id);\n  render();\n}\n\nfunction toggleAction(event, stepId, actionIdx) {\n  event.stopPropagation();\n  const key = `${stepId}:${actionIdx}`;\n  checkedActions[key] = !checkedActions[key];\n  event.currentTarget.classList.toggle('checked', checkedActions[key]);\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction toggleNotes() {\n  notesVisible = !notesVisible;\n  document.querySelectorAll('.notes').forEach(note => note.classList.toggle('hidden', !notesVisible));\n  updateToggleButtons();\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction toggleQA() {\n  qasVisible = !qasVisible;\n  document.querySelectorAll('.qa-section').forEach(section => section.classList.toggle('hidden', !qasVisible));\n  updateToggleButtons();\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction togglePartQa(partNumber) {\n  partQaOpen[partNumber] = !partQaOpen[partNumber];\n  render();\n  document.getElementById(`part-qa-${partNumber}`)?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });\n}\n\nfunction goToPartQA(partNumber) {\n  qasVisible = true;\n  partQaOpen[partNumber] = true;\n  render();\n  document.getElementById(`part-qa-${partNumber}`)?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', block: 'start' });\n}\n\nfunction toggleTimer() {\n  if (timerRunning) {\n    clearInterval(timerInterval);\n    timerRunning = false;\n    updateTimerDisplay();\n    safeSaveState();\n    return;\n  }\n\n  timerRunning = true;\n  updateTimerDisplay();\n  timerInterval = setInterval(() => {\n    timerSeconds += 1;\n    updateTimerDisplay();\n    safeSaveState();\n  }, 1000);\n}\n\nfunction openKnowledgeBase(id) {\n  if (id) selectedKnowledgeId = id;\n  document.getElementById('knowledgeModal').classList.add('show');\n  renderKnowledgeBase();\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction switchKnowledge(id) {\n  selectedKnowledgeId = id;\n  renderKnowledgeBase();\n  safeSaveState();\n}\n\nfunction closeKnowledgeBase() {\n  document.getElementById('knowledgeModal').classList.remove('show');\n}\n\nfunction toggleShortcuts(forceOpen) {\n  const modal = document.getElementById('shortcutsModal');\n  if (typeof forceOpen === 'boolean') {\n    modal.classList.toggle('show', forceOpen);\n  } else {\n    modal.classList.toggle('show');\n  }\n}\n\nfunction closeModals() {\n  document.getElementById('knowledgeModal').classList.remove('show');\n  document.getElementById('shortcutsModal').classList.remove('show');\n}\n\nfunction resetAll() {\n  if (!confirm('Reset all progress, action checks, toggles, and timer?')) return;\n  completedSteps.clear();\n  checkedActions = {};\n  partQaOpen = Object.fromEntries(DEMO.map(part => [part.part, false]));\n  notesVisible = true;\n  qasVisible = true;\n  currentStepIdx = 0;\n  selectedKnowledgeId = KNOWLEDGE_BASE[0]?.id || selectedKnowledgeId;\n  timerSeconds = 0;\n  timerRunning = false;\n  clearInterval(timerInterval);\n  closeModals();\n  safeSaveState();\n  render();\n  window.scrollTo({ top: 0, behavior: 'smooth' });\n}\n\ndocument.addEventListener('keydown', event => {\n  if (event.target && ['INPUT', 'TEXTAREA', 'SELECT'].includes(event.target.tagName)) return;\n  switch (event.key) {\n    case 'ArrowRight':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      nextStep();\n      break;\n    case 'ArrowLeft':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      prevStep();\n      break;\n    case 'h':\n    case 'H':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      toggleNotes();\n      break;\n    case 'q':\n    case 'Q':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      toggleQA();\n      break;\n    case 't':\n    case 'T':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      toggleTimer();\n      break;\n    case 'd':\n    case 'D':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      markDone();\n      break;\n    case 'k':\n    case 'K':\n      event.preventDefault();\n      openKnowledgeBase();\n      break;\n    case 'Escape':\n      closeModals();\n      break;\n  }\n});\n\nloadState();\nrender();\n</script>\n</body>\n</html>\n'''\n\nhtml = template.replace('__KB_DATA__', json.dumps(knowledge_base, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))\nhtml = html.replace('__QA_DATA__', json.dumps(qa_by_part, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))\nhtml = html.replace('__DEMO_DATA__', demo_source)\n\npath.unlink()\npath.write_text(html, encoding='utf-8')\nprint(f'Wrote enhanced guide to {path}')\n'@ | python -",
  "description": "Replace demo guide with enhanced self-contained HTML",
  "initial_wait": 30
}
```

await (async (page) => {
  const result = {};

  result.qToggleText = await page.locator('#qaBtn').innerText();
  await page.evaluate(() => document.querySelector('#qaBtn').click());

  await page.keyboard.press('t');
  return result;
})(page);

Let me think about what would be most useful as a "demo webpage" shown to the customer:

1. An interactive, visually appealing page that explains the Fabric CI/CD architecture
2. Shows the schema gap visually with animations
3. Shows the solution (schema-as-code) with interactive diagrams
4. Has live code examples they can walk through
5. Architecture diagrams using CSS/SVG


6. A comparison table showing the differences between Lakehouse and Warehouse approaches
7. An interactive pipeline visualization that demonstrates the Dev → UAT → Prod workflow

I should build something polished and professional that they can display to customers, distinct from the presenter-facing demo guide with its internal notes and checkboxes.*

{
  "summary": "Created `demo-presentation.html` — a polished, customer-facing interactive presentation with 12 slides:\n\n1. **Title** — CI/CD for Microsoft Fabric, branded for Digital Realty\n2. **Platform** — Fabric overview with Lakehouse/Warehouse/Models/Pipelines cards + \"What is a Lakehouse?\" explainer\n3. **Git Tracking** — Side-by-side ✅ tracked vs ❌ not-tracked table with warning callout\n4. **The Gap** — Flow diagram (Dev → Git → UAT), real-world impact timeline, 4 consequences\n5. **Solution** — Schema-as-Code flow with Versioned/Idempotent/Automated cards + industry-standard callout\n6. **Pipeline** — Dev → UAT → Prod deployment pipeline with Track 1 vs Track 2 comparison\n7. **Migration Scripts** — Actual SQL code for v001 baseline and v002 evolution side-by-side\n8. **Runner** — 5-step process diagram + Python code showing the migration runner notebook\n9. **CI/CD** — Azure DevOps YAML + GitHub Actions YAML side-by-side with auth/approval details\n10. **APIs** — REST endpoint table + live Python code example\n11. **Architecture** — Full Track 1 (standard) vs Track 2 (schema) comparison + Lakehouse vs Warehouse guidance\n12. **Summary** — 3 pillars + Next Steps + documentation links\n\nFeatures: keyboard navigation (←/→), nav bar with slide counter, scroll-snap, scroll-reveal animations, dark theme with Microsoft branding. Fully self-contained HTML — just open in a browser."
}

<details>
<summary>14 lines</summary>

```
Created `demo-presentation.html` — a polished, customer-facing interactive presentation with 12 slides:

1. **Title** — CI/CD for Microsoft Fabric, branded for Digital Realty
2. **Platform** — Fabric overview with Lakehouse/Warehouse/Models/Pipelines cards + "What is a Lakehouse?" explainer
3. **Git Tracking** — Side-by-side ✅ tracked vs ❌ not-tracked table with warning callout
4. **The Gap** — Flow diagram (Dev → Git → UAT), real-world impact timeline, 4 consequences
5. **Solution** — Schema-as-Code flow with Versioned/Idempotent/Automated cards + industry-standard callout
6. **Pipeline** — Dev → UAT → Prod deployment pipeline with Track 1 vs Track 2 comparison

8. **Runner** — 5-step process diagram + Python code showing the migration runner notebook
9. **CI/CD** — Azure DevOps YAML + GitHub Actions YAML side-by-side with auth/approval details
10. **APIs** — REST endpoint table + live Python code example
11. **Architecture** — Full Track 1 (standard) vs Track 2 (schema) comparison + Lakehouse vs Warehouse guidance
12. **Summary** — 3 pillars + Next Steps + documentation links

Features: keyboard navigation (←/→), nav bar with slide counter, scroll-snap, scroll-reveal animations, dark theme with Microsoft branding. Fully self-contained HTML — just open in a browser.

<sub>⏱️ 2873m 17s</sub>

### 👤 User

[📷 copilot-image-5996ce.png] based on this explanation we are running a big risk since GIthub does not track tables, views , schemas, defitions, stored procedures, table data so based on this feature this is going to break their pipelines since tables will persist or stroed by Github so we have a big gap . How do we tak care of that gap and give customers a way to save tables , views, schemas and stored procedures. can we do something about it until it comes in the product like github actions workflow. we are showing the problem but no solution or a alternative path to a solution

---

<sub>⏱️ 2873m 20s</sub>

### 💭 Reasoning

*The user is looking at the demo presentation slide that shows the Git tracking gap for Lakehouse artifacts. They're saying that while we've identified the problem clearly, we need a much stronger solution section. They want:

1. A concrete workaround/solution for the gap (tables, views, schemas, stored procedures, column definitions not being tracked)
2. Something actionable like a GitHub Actions workflow that can bridge this gap
3. Not just showing the problem but showing a path to solution

We already have the schema-as-code migration approach in the repo, but the user wants something more comprehensive. Let me think about what we can build:

**The Solution Architecture:**

1. **Schema Export Workflow** - A GitHub Actions workflow (or Azure DevOps pipeline) that:
   - Connects to the Lakehouse SQL Analytics Endpoint
   - Extracts current schema (tables, views, columns, stored procs) using INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries
   - Saves the DDL as SQL files in Git
   - Auto-commits the changes
   - This creates a "schema snapshot" that gets version-controlled

2. **Schema Apply Workflow** - A complementary workflow that reads the schema files from Git, applies them to target environments, and handles drift detection by comparing current versus desired state

3. **Schema Drift Detection** - A scheduled workflow that periodically checks for untracked changes and alerts when the actual schema diverges from what's in Git

4. **Migration Runner Enhancement** - Update the existing migration runner to handle views, stored procedures, and schema comparisons

Now I'm planning the implementation approach. I need to update the presentation with stronger solution slides, create the actual GitHub Actions workflows, build out the schema export and import scripts, and update the demo guide with the full narrative. The core pieces are a schema extractor that pulls all DDL from the Lakehouse endpoint, a schema applier that deploys those definitions to target environments, and a drift detector that continuously monitors for manual changes.

For querying the Lakehouse SQL Analytics Endpoint, I can leverage T-SQL system views like INFORMATION_SCHEMA and sys.objects to extract table definitions, columns, views, and stored procedures.

Now I'm considering the different approaches to solve this: Schema-as-Code with versioned migrations, automated schema export that captures the actual state to Git, or a diff-and-apply strategy that compares environments and generates promotion scripts automatically. Each has tradeoffs between control, automation, and safety.

For the gold layer specifically, using Fabric Warehouse instead of Lakehouse gives native serialization support for tables, views, and functions, which eliminates the need for workarounds. The real solution is combining these approaches—migrations for intentional changes, automated exports to catch ad-hoc modifications, and drift detection as a safety mechanism. I'll build out the schema extractor, drift detector, and GitHub Actions workflows to support this, then update the presentation and demo guide with these recommendations.*

---

<sub>⏱️ 2873m 47s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot



You're absolutely right — showing the gap without a concrete solution leaves the customer hanging. Let me build a complete **Schema Bridge** solution with actual working scripts and CI/CD workflows that close this gap.
